491	          whileInView={{ opacity: 1, y: 0 }}
492	          viewport={{ once: true, amount: 0.3 }}
493	          transition={{
494	            duration: 0.6,
495	            delay: i * 0.06,
496	            ease: [0.16, 1, 0.3, 1],
497	          }}
498	        >
499	          {item}
500	        </motion.li>
501	      ))}
502	    </ul>
503	  );
504	}
505	```
506	
507	Use this for: feature lists, testimonial grids, logo walls, anything that just needs "enter on scroll." Save GSAP for actual pin/scrub work.
508	
509	### 5.D Forbidden Animation Patterns
510	
511	* **`window.addEventListener("scroll", ...)`** is banned. It runs on every scroll frame, jank-prone, no batching. Use Motion's `useScroll()`, GSAP's `ScrollTrigger`, IntersectionObserver, or CSS `scroll-driven animations` (`animation-timeline: view()`).
512	* **Custom scroll progress calculations using `window.scrollY`** in React state. Same reason. Re-renders on every frame.
513	* **`requestAnimationFrame` loops that touch React state.** Use motion values (`useMotionValue` + `useTransform`) instead.
514	* **Layout Transitions:** Use Motion's `layout` and `layoutId` props for visible state changes (re-ordering lists, expanding modals, shared elements between routes). Do not wrap static content in `layout` props "for safety" - it costs measurement work.
515	* **Staggered Orchestration:** Use `staggerChildren` (Motion) or CSS cascade (`animation-delay: calc(var(--index) * 100ms)`) for reveal moments where sequence matters. For `staggerChildren`, parent (`variants`) and children MUST share the same Client Component tree.
516	
517	---
518	
519	## 6. PERFORMANCE & ACCESSIBILITY GUARDRAILS
520	
521	### 6.A Hardware Acceleration
522	* Animate ONLY `transform` and `opacity`. Never animate `top`, `left`, `width`, `height`.
523	* Use `will-change: transform` sparingly - only on elements that will actually animate.
524	
525	### 6.B Reduced Motion (mandatory)
526	* **Any motion above `MOTION_INTENSITY > 3` MUST honor `prefers-reduced-motion`.** This is non-negotiable.
527	* In Motion: wrap with `useReducedMotion()` and degrade to static.
528	* In CSS: gate animations behind `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: no-preference)` or provide an override block under `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` that disables.
529	* Infinite loops, parallax, scroll-hijack, and magnetic physics MUST collapse to static / instant under reduced motion.
530	
531	### 6.C Dark Mode (mandatory for any consumer-facing page)
532	* Design for **both modes from the start**. Never ship light-only or dark-only without explicit user instruction.
533	* Use Tailwind `dark:` variant OR CSS variables for tokens. Pick one strategy per project.
534	* **Do not prescribe specific dark-mode colors here.** The brief decides. Maintain visual hierarchy, brand identity, and WCAG AA contrast (AAA for body) across both modes.
535	* Respect `prefers-color-scheme: dark`. Default to system preference unless the brand insists on one mode.
536	
537	### 6.D Core Web Vitals Targets
538	* **LCP** < 2.5s. Hero image must be `next/image priority` or preloaded.
539	* **INP** < 200ms. Heavy work off main thread.
540	* **CLS** < 0.1. Reserve space for images, fonts, embeds.
541	* Run Lighthouse before declaring a page done.
542	
543	### 6.E DOM Cost
544	* Apply grain / noise filters EXCLUSIVELY to fixed, `pointer-events-none` pseudo-elements (e.g., `fixed inset-0 z-[60] pointer-events-none`). NEVER on scrolling containers - continuous GPU repaints destroy mobile FPS.
545	* Be aware of bundle size. Motion is not tiny. Three.js is large. Lazy-load anything that's not above-the-fold.
546	
547	### 6.F Z-Index Restraint
548	NEVER spam arbitrary `z-50` or `z-10`. Use z-index strictly for systemic layer contexts (sticky navbars, modals, overlays, grain). Document the z-index scale in a project constants file.
549	
550	---
551	
552	## 7. DIAL DEFINITIONS (Technical Reference)
553	
554	### DESIGN_VARIANCE (Level 1-10)
555	* **1-3 (Predictable):** Symmetrical CSS Grid (12-col, equal fr-units), equal paddings, centered alignment.
556	* **4-7 (Offset):** `margin-top: -2rem` overlaps, varied image aspect ratios (4:3 next to 16:9), left-aligned headers over center-aligned data.
557	* **8-10 (Asymmetric):** Masonry layouts, CSS Grid with fractional units (`grid-template-columns: 2fr 1fr 1fr`), massive empty zones (`padding-left: 20vw`).
558	* **MOBILE OVERRIDE:** For levels 4-10, asymmetric layouts above `md:` MUST collapse to strict single-column (`w-full`, `px-4`, `py-8`) on viewports `< 768px`.
559	
560	### MOTION_INTENSITY (Level 1-10)
561	* **1-3 (Static):** No automatic animations. CSS `:hover` and `:active` states only. `prefers-reduced-motion` is the default mode anyway.
562	* **4-7 (Fluid CSS):** `transition: all 0.3s cubic-bezier(0.16, 1, 0.3, 1)`. `animation-delay` cascades for load-ins. Focus on `transform` and `opacity`.
563	* **8-10 (Advanced Choreography):** Complex scroll-triggered reveals, parallax, scroll-driven animation (CSS `animation-timeline` or GSAP ScrollTrigger). Use Motion hooks. **NEVER use `window.addEventListener('scroll')`** - it is a hard ban, not a "prefer-not." See Section 5.D for the allowed alternatives.
564	
565	### VISUAL_DENSITY (Level 1-10)
566	* **1-3 (Art Gallery):** Lots of white space. Huge section gaps (`py-32` to `py-48`). Expensive, clean.
567	* **4-7 (Daily App):** Standard web app spacing (`py-16` to `py-24`).
568	* **8-10 (Cockpit):** Tight paddings. No card boxes; 1px lines separate data. Mandatory: `font-mono` for all numbers.
569	
570	---
571	
572	## 8. DARK MODE PROTOCOL
573	
574	Dual-mode by default. Never assume light-only unless the brief is print-emulating editorial.
575	
576	### 8.A Token Strategy (pick one, stick to it)
577	* **Tailwind `dark:` variant** (default for utility-first projects): every color utility paired with its dark variant (`bg-white dark:bg-zinc-950`, `text-gray-900 dark:text-gray-100`).
578	* **CSS variables** (for shadcn/ui, Radix Themes, or component libraries with theming): define semantic tokens (`--surface`, `--surface-elevated`, `--text-primary`, `--accent`) and swap values under `[data-theme="dark"]` or `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)`.
579	
580	### 8.B Do Not Prescribe Specific Colors Here
581	The brief and brand decide. This skill enforces only:
582	* **Contrast** - WCAG AA minimum for body text, AAA target for hero copy.
583	* **Hierarchy parity** - visual hierarchy that works in light must work in dark. If a CTA pops in light, it pops in dark.
584	* **Brand fidelity** - primary brand color stays recognisable. Don't desaturate the brand into a dark mode.
585	* **No pure `#000000` and no pure `#ffffff`** - use off-black (zinc-950, near-black warm gray) and off-white. Pure values kill depth.
586	
587	### 8.C Default Mode
588	Respect `prefers-color-scheme` unless the brand insists. Add a manual toggle if either mode would lose key brand expression.
589	
590	### 8.D Test in Both Modes Before Finishing
591	Open the page in both modes during development. Do not ship a page you've only seen in one mode.
592	
593	---
594	
595	## 9. AI TELLS (Forbidden Patterns)
596	
597	Avoid these signatures unless the brief explicitly asks for them.
598	
599	### 9.A Visual & CSS
600	* **NO neon / outer glows** by default. Use inner borders or subtle tinted shadows.
601	* **NO pure black (`#000000`).** Off-black, zinc-950, or charcoal.
602	* **NO oversaturated accents.** Desaturate to blend with neutrals.
603	* **NO excessive gradient text** for large headers.
604	* **NO custom mouse cursors.** Outdated, accessibility-hostile, perf-hostile.
605	
606	### 9.B Typography
607	* **AVOID Inter as default.** See Section 4.1. Override path exists.
608	* **NO oversized H1s** that just scream. Control hierarchy with weight + color, not raw scale.
609	* **Serif constraints:** Serif for editorial / luxury / publication. Not for dashboards.
610	
611	### 9.C Layout & Spacing
612	* **Mathematically perfect** padding and margins. No floating elements with awkward gaps.
613	* **NO 3-column equal feature cards.** The generic "three identical cards horizontally" feature row is banned. Use 2-column zig-zag, asymmetric grid, scroll-pinned, or horizontal-scroll alternative.
614	
615	### 9.D Content & Data ("Jane Doe" Effect)
616	* **NO generic names.** "John Doe", "Sarah Chan", "Jack Su" → use creative, realistic, locale-appropriate names.
617	* **NO generic avatars.** No SVG "egg" or Lucide user icons → use believable photo placeholders or specific styling.
618	* **NO fake-perfect numbers.** Avoid `99.99%`, `50%`, `1234567`. Use organic, messy data (`47.2%`, `+1 (312) 847-1928`).
619	* **NO startup-slop brand names.** "Acme", "Nexus", "SmartFlow", "Cloudly" → invent contextual, premium names that sound real.
620	* **NO filler verbs.** "Elevate", "Seamless", "Unleash", "Next-Gen", "Revolutionize" → concrete verbs only.
621	
622	### 9.E External Resources & Components
623	* **NO hand-rolled SVG icons.** Use Phosphor / HugeIcons / Radix / Tabler. Lucide on explicit request only.
624	* **Hand-rolled decorative SVGs strongly discouraged** as default (see Section 4.8).
625	* **NO div-based fake screenshots.** Never build a fake product UI out of `<div>` rectangles to simulate a screenshot. Use real images, generated images, or skip the preview.
626	* **NO broken Unsplash links.** Use `https://picsum.photos/seed/{descriptive-string}/{w}/{h}`, or generated photo placeholders, or actual assets.
627	* **shadcn/ui customization:** Allowed, but NEVER in default state. Customize radii, colors, shadows, typography to the project aesthetic.
628	* **Production-Ready Cleanliness:** Code visually clean, memorable, meticulously refined.
629	
630	### 9.F Production-Test Tells (banned outright)
631	
632	These patterns came out of real LLM-generated landing-page tests. They are the signatures the model defaults to when it tries to "look designed." Treat them as hard bans unless the brief explicitly calls for one.
633	
634	**Hero & top-of-page**
635	* **NO version labels in the hero.** `V0.6`, `v2.0`, `BETA`, `INVITE-ONLY PREVIEW`, `EARLY ACCESS`, `ALPHA` - banned as default eyebrows. Only acceptable when the brief is explicitly about a product launch / preview status.
636	* **NO "Brand · No. 01"-style sub-eyebrows.** "Marrow · No. 01 · The 6-quart" type micro-meta lines. Skip them.
637	
638	**Section numbering & micro-labels**
639	* **NO section-number eyebrows.** `00 / INDEX`, `001 · Capabilities`, `002 · Featured commission`, `06 · how it works`, `05 · The honest table` - banned. Eyebrows should name the topic in plain language, not enumerate.
640	* **NO `01 / 4`-style pagination on images or bento tiles.** If the user can count, they don't need the label.
641	* **NO `Scroll · 001 Capabilities`-style scroll cues.** A simple arrow or "Scroll" is enough; no section-number prefix.
642	* **NO "Index of Work, 2018 - 2026"-style range labels** as eyebrows. Just say what the section is.
643	
644	**Separators & dots**
645	* **The middle-dot (`·`) is rationed.** Maximum 1 per line in metadata strips. Do NOT use it as the default separator for everything ("foo · bar · baz · qux · quux"). If you need a separator family, prefer line breaks, hairlines, or columns.
646	* **NO decorative colored status dots on every list/nav/badge.** A colored dot before "ONE Q4 SLOT OPEN" or before every nav link, or every task row - banned by default. Acceptable only when the dot conveys actual semantic state (a server status, an availability flag) and is used sparingly.
647	
648	**Em-dashes & typography flourishes**
649	* **NO em-dash (`—`) as a design element OR anywhere else.** See Section 9.G below for the complete, non-negotiable ban. The em-dash character is forbidden in headlines, eyebrows, pills, body copy, quotes, attribution, captions, button text, and alt text. Use the regular hyphen (`-`).
650	* **NO `<br>`-broken-and-italicized headlines** as a default "design move." "for thirty\<br\>*years.*" type splits. Headlines should read naturally first, get clever only when the brief demands it.
651	* **NO vertical rotated text** ("INDEX OF WORK, 2018 - 2026" rotated 90°). Agency-portfolio cliché. Use it only when the brief is explicitly agency / Awwwards / experimental AND it serves a real composition purpose.
652	* **NO crosshair / hairline grid lines as decoration.** Vertical and horizontal lines drawn just to make the page "feel designed" - banned. Use them only when they organize real content.
653	
654	**Fake product previews**
655	* **NO div-based fake product UI in the hero** (fake task list, fake terminal, fake dashboard built from styled divs). It is the #1 LLM-design Tell. Use a real screenshot, a generated image, a real component preview, or none at all.
656	* **NO fake version footers** ("v0.6.2-rc.1", "last sync 4s ago · main") inside fake screenshots. Adds nothing, screams AI.
657	
658	**Marketing-copy Tells**
659	* **NO "Quietly in use at" / "Quietly trusted by"** social-proof headers. Use natural language: "Trusted by", "Used at", "Customers include", or skip the heading entirely if the logos speak.
660	* **NO "From the field" / "Field notes" / "Currently on the bench" / "On our desks" / "Loose plates" style poetic labels** on quote, blog, or sidebar sections. Reads as performative-craftsman. Use plain functional labels ("Testimonials", "Latest writing", "Now working on") or skip the label.
661	* **NO "We respect the French ones"-style** mock-humble industry-references in body copy. Cute and AI-y.
662	* **NO weather / locale strips** ("LIS 14:23 · 18°C") in headers/footers unless the brief is explicitly about a place / time-zone-distributed studio.
663	* **NO micro-meta-sentences under eyebrows.** Sentences like *"Each of these is a feature we ship today, not a roadmap promise. The list will stay short on purpose."* sitting under a section heading are clutter. Eyebrow + Headline + Body is enough.
664	* **NO generic step labels.** "Stage 1 / Stage 2 / Stage 3", "Step 1 / Step 2 / Step 3", "Phase 01 / Phase 02 / Phase 03", "Pass One / Pass Two / Pass Three". Banned. The actual step content is the label. If you must show progression, use the verb-noun directly ("Install", "Configure", "Ship") not "Stage 1: Install".
665	
666	**Pills, labels and version stamps**
667	* **NO pills/labels/tags overlaid on images.** No `<span>` overlays on photos with tags like `Brand · 02`, `PLATE · BRAND`, `Field notes - journal`. Either let the image speak alone, or add a caption directly below (outside the image).
668	* **NO photo-credit captions as decoration.** Strings like `Field study no. 12 · Ines Caetano`, `Plate 03 · House archive`, `Frame XII · 35mm` under stock/picsum images are pretentious. Photo credit is allowed ONLY when there is a real photographer being credited for a real photo (with permission). Otherwise: skip the caption or use a one-line functional caption ("The 6-quart, in Sage.").
669	* **NO version footers on marketing pages.** Footer strings like `v1.4.2`, `Build 0048`, `last sync 4s ago · main` are CLI / devtool fixtures, not landing-page content. Banned on marketing/landing/portfolio pages.
670	* **NO "Reservation 412 of 800"-style live-stock counters** as decoration. Only if the brief is explicitly a limited-run waitlist with real data.
671	
672	**Decoration text strips**
673	* **NO decoration text strip at hero bottom.** Patterns like `BRAND. MOTION. SPATIAL.`, `TYPE / FORM / MOTION`, `DESIGN · BUILD · SHIP`, `ESTD. 2018 · LISBON · BRAND. MOTION. SPATIAL.` as a small mono-caps strip across the bottom of the hero are an agency-portfolio cliché. Banned by default. Only acceptable when the strip carries real, navigable links (sticky bottom nav) or real status info (cookie banner, build info on a docs site).
674	* **NO floating top-right sub-text in section headings.** Pattern: section has a giant left-aligned headline; in the top-right corner of the same section header there is a small explainer paragraph floating with no clear alignment to anything else. That floater is the Tell. Either put the sub-text directly under the headline, or build a clean 2-column header (left: headline, right: aligned body), but not a tiny corner paragraph.
675	
676	**Lists, dividers and scoring**
677	* **NO `border-t` + `border-b` on every row of a long list / spec table.** Pick one (bottom-border between rows OR top-border above the group) and use it sparsely. A 10-row spec table with hairlines under each row is the laziest layout - see Section 4.9 for alternative UI components.
678	* **NO scoring/progress bars with filled background tracks** as comparison visuals. If you need to show "X out of Y" comparisons, prefer a number + small icon, or a tiny inline bar WITHOUT a background track. Big filled `bg-zinc-200` tracks with a partial fill on top are dashboard-UI clutter on a landing page.
679	
680	**Locale, time, scroll cues**
681	* **Locale / city-name / time / weather strips are banned for 99% of briefs.** "Lisbon, working with founders" in the hero, "1200-690 Lisbon, Portugal" in the footer, "Lisbon 14:23 · 18°C" in the nav. These are agency-portfolio decoration tells. Allowed ONLY when: the brief explicitly describes a globally-distributed studio with timezone-relevant work, OR a travel-focused brand, OR a real-world physical venue. A single contact-address mention in the footer is fine; an atmospheric locale strip is not.
682	* **Scroll cues are banned.** `Scroll`, `↓ scroll`, `Scroll to explore`, `Scroll to walk through it`, animated mouse-wheel icons. If the user has not scrolled yet, they are looking at the hero. They know what scroll is. The bottom of the viewport does not need a label.
683	* **ZERO decorative status dots by default.** A coloured dot before nav items, before list rows, before badges, before status labels is a Tell. Only acceptable when conveying real semantic state (a live indicator on actual server status, a live availability flag) and limited to one per page section.
684	
685	### 9.G EM-DASH BAN (the single most-violated Tell)
686	
687	**Em-dash (`—`) is COMPLETELY banned.** It is the LLM's signature stylistic crutch and it is the #1 visual Tell in production tests. There is no "limited use" allowance, no "natural language frequency" allowance, no "in body copy is fine" allowance. None.
688	
689	* **Banned in headlines.** Use a period or a comma.
690	* **Banned in eyebrows / labels / pills / button text / image captions / nav items.** Replace with line breaks, columns, or hairlines.
691	* **Banned in body copy.** Restructure the sentence: two sentences with a period, OR a comma, OR parentheses, OR a colon.
692	* **Banned in quote attribution.** Use a normal hyphen with spaces (` - `) or a line break + smaller-weight name.
693	* **Banned in en-dash form too (`–`) when used as a separator.** Date ranges (`2018-2026`) use a hyphen. Number ranges (`€40-80k`) use a hyphen.
694	
695	The ONLY permitted dash characters on the page are:
696	* Regular hyphen `-` (for compound words, ranges, line dividers in markup)
697	* Minus sign in math (`-5°C`)
698	
699	If your output contains a single `—` or `–` anywhere visible to the user, the output fails the Pre-Flight Check and must be rewritten.
700	
701	This rule is non-negotiable. The agent has historically ignored em-dash limits when phrased as "use sparingly." The phrasing here is binary: zero em-dashes.
702	
703	---
704	
705	## 10. REFERENCE VOCABULARY (Pattern Names the Agent Should Know)
706	
707	This is a vocabulary, not a library. The agent should KNOW these pattern names to communicate about them, design with them in mind, and reach for them when the design read calls for them. **Implementations and code sketches live in the Block Library (Section 12), which is populated iteratively.**
708	
709	### Hero Paradigms
710	* **Asymmetric Split Hero** - Text on one side, asset on the other, generous white space.
711	* **Editorial Manifesto Hero** - Large type, no asset, almost-poster.
712	* **Video / Media Mask Hero** - Type cut out as mask over video background.
713	* **Kinetic-Type Hero** - Animated typography as the primary visual.
714	* **Curtain-Reveal Hero** - Hero parts on scroll like a curtain.
715	* **Scroll-Pinned Hero** - Hero stays pinned while content scrolls behind.
716	
717	### Navigation & Menus
718	* **Mac OS Dock Magnification** - Edge nav, icons scale fluidly on hover.
719	* **Magnetic Button** - Pulls toward cursor.
720	* **Gooey Menu** - Sub-items detach like viscous liquid.
721	* **Dynamic Island** - Morphing pill for status / alerts.
722	* **Contextual Radial Menu** - Circular menu expanding at click point.
723	* **Floating Speed Dial** - FAB springing into curved secondary actions.
724	* **Mega Menu Reveal** - Full-screen dropdown, stagger-fade content.
725	
726	### Layout & Grids
727	* **Bento Grid** - Asymmetric tile grouping (Apple Control Center).
728	* **Masonry Layout** - Staggered grid, no fixed row height.
729	* **Chroma Grid** - Borders / tiles with subtle animating gradients.
730	* **Split-Screen Scroll** - Two halves sliding in opposite directions.
731	* **Sticky-Stack Sections** - Sections that pin and stack on scroll.
732	
733	### Cards & Containers
734	* **Parallax Tilt Card** - 3D tilt tracking mouse coordinates.
735	* **Spotlight Border Card** - Borders illuminate under cursor.
736	* **Glassmorphism Panel** - Frosted glass with inner refraction.
737	* **Holographic Foil Card** - Iridescent rainbow shift on hover.
738	* **Tinder Swipe Stack** - Physical card stack, swipe-away.
739	* **Morphing Modal** - Button expands into its own dialog.
740	
741	### Scroll Animations
742	* **Sticky Scroll Stack** - Cards stick and physically stack.
743	* **Horizontal Scroll Hijack** - Vertical scroll → horizontal pan.
744	* **Locomotive / Sequence Scroll** - Video / 3D sequence tied to scrollbar.
745	* **Zoom Parallax** - Central background image zooming on scroll.
746	* **Scroll Progress Path** - SVG line drawing along scroll.
747	* **Liquid Swipe Transition** - Page transition like viscous liquid.
748	
749	### Galleries & Media
750	* **Dome Gallery** - 3D panoramic gallery.
751	* **Coverflow Carousel** - 3D carousel with angled edges.
752	* **Drag-to-Pan Grid** - Boundless draggable canvas.
753	* **Accordion Image Slider** - Narrow strips expanding on hover.
754	* **Hover Image Trail** - Mouse leaves popping image trail.
755	* **Glitch Effect Image** - RGB-channel shift on hover.
756	
757	### Typography & Text
758	* **Kinetic Marquee** - Endless text bands reversing on scroll.
759	* **Text Mask Reveal** - Massive type as transparent window to video.
760	* **Text Scramble Effect** - Matrix-style decoding on load / hover.
761	* **Circular Text Path** - Text curving along spinning circle.
762	* **Gradient Stroke Animation** - Outlined text with running gradient.
763	* **Kinetic Typography Grid** - Letters dodging the cursor.
764	
765	### Micro-Interactions & Effects
766	* **Particle Explosion Button** - CTA shatters into particles on success.
767	* **Liquid Pull-to-Refresh** - Reload indicator like detaching droplets.
768	* **Skeleton Shimmer** - Shifting light reflection across placeholders.
769	* **Directional Hover-Aware Button** - Fill enters from cursor's exact side.
770	* **Ripple Click Effect** - Wave from click coordinates.
771	* **Animated SVG Line Drawing** - Vectors drawing themselves in real time.
772	* **Mesh Gradient Background** - Organic lava-lamp blobs.
773	* **Lens Blur Depth** - Background UI blurred to focus foreground action.
774	
775	### Animation Library Choice
776	* **Motion (`motion/react`)** - default for UI / Bento / state-change motion.
777	* **GSAP + ScrollTrigger** - for full-page scrolltelling and scroll hijacks. Isolate in dedicated leaf components with `useEffect` cleanup.
778	* **Three.js / WebGL** - for canvas backgrounds and 3D scenes. Same isolation rule.
779	* **NEVER mix GSAP / Three.js with Motion in the same component tree.** They fight over the same frames.
780	
781	---
782	
783	## 11. REDESIGN PROTOCOL
784	
785	This skill handles **greenfield builds AND redesigns**. Misclassifying the mode is the single biggest source of bad redesign output.
786	
787	### 11.A Detect the Mode (first action)
788	* **Greenfield** - no existing site, or full overhaul approved. Dial baseline from Section 1.
789	* **Redesign - Preserve** - modernise without breaking the brand. Audit first, extract brand tokens, evolve gradually.
790	* **Redesign - Overhaul** - new visual language on top of existing content. Treat as greenfield for visuals; preserve content and IA.
791	
792	If ambiguous, ask **once**: *"Should this redesign preserve the existing brand, or are we starting visually from scratch?"*
793	
794	### 11.B Audit Before Touching
795	Document the current state before proposing changes:
796	* **Brand tokens** - primary / accent colors, type stack, logo treatment, radii.
797	* **Information architecture** - page tree, primary nav, key conversion paths.
798	* **Content blocks** - what exists, what's doing work, what's filler.
799	* **Patterns to preserve** - signature interactions, recognisable hero, copy voice.
800	* **Patterns to retire** - AI-slop tells, broken layouts, dead links, generic stock imagery, perf traps.
801	* **Dial reading of the existing site** - infer current `DESIGN_VARIANCE` / `MOTION_INTENSITY` / `VISUAL_DENSITY`. That's your starting point, not the baseline.
802	* **SEO baseline** - current ranking pages, meta titles, structured data, OG cards. **SEO migration is the #1 redesign risk.**
803	
804	### 11.C Preservation Rules
805	* **Do not change information architecture** unless asked. Keep page slugs, anchor IDs, primary nav labels stable for SEO and muscle memory.
806	* **Extract brand colors before applying Section 4.2.** A brand that is already purple stays purple - apply the LILA RULE's override.
807	* **Preserve copy voice** unless asked for a rewrite. Visual modernisation ≠ content rewrite.
808	* **Honor existing accessibility wins.** Do not regress focus states, alt text, keyboard nav, contrast.
809	* **Respect existing analytics events.** Do not rename buttons, form fields, section IDs that downstream tracking depends on.
810	
811	### 11.D Modernisation Levers (priority order)
812	Apply in order - stop when the brief is satisfied:
813	1. **Typography refresh** - biggest visual lift per unit of risk.
814	2. **Spacing & rhythm** - increase section padding, fix vertical rhythm.
815	3. **Color recalibration** - desaturate, unify neutrals, keep brand accent.
816	4. **Motion layer** - add `MOTION_INTENSITY`-appropriate micro-interactions to existing components.
817	5. **Hero & key-section recomposition** - restructure top-of-funnel using Section 10 vocabulary.
818	6. **Full block replacement** - only when the existing block is unsalvageable.
819	
820	### 11.E Decision Tree: Targeted Evolution vs Full Redesign
821	* IA, content, and SEO sound → **targeted evolution** (Levers 1-4). ~70% of value at ~40% of risk.
822	* Visual debt is structural (broken IA, no design system, broken mobile) → **full redesign** with strict content preservation.
823	* Brand itself is changing → **greenfield**.
824	
825	### 11.F What Never Changes Silently
826	Never modify without explicit user approval:
827	* URL structure / route slugs.
828	* Primary nav labels.
829	* Form field names or order (breaks analytics + autofill).
830	* Brand logo or wordmark.
831	* Existing legal / consent / cookie copy.
832	
833	---
834	
835	## 12. THE BLOCK LIBRARY (Contract - Implementations Land Here Iteratively)
836	
837	The Reference Vocabulary (Section 10) names patterns. The Block Library implements them with real props, real motion specs, and real code sketches.
838	
839	**Status:** schema defined here. Blocks will be added iteratively. Do not freelance new blocks without following this schema.
840	
841	### 12.A File Location
842	```
843	skills/taste-skill/blocks/
844	  hero/
845	    asymmetric-split.md
846	    editorial-manifesto.md
847	    kinetic-type.md
848	    ...
849	  feature/
850	    bento-grid.md
851	    sticky-scroll-stack.md
852	    zig-zag.md
853	    ...
854	  social-proof/
855	  pricing/
856	  cta/
857	  footer/
858	  navigation/
859	  portfolio/
860	  transition/
861	```
862	
863	### 12.B Required Frontmatter
864	```yaml
865	---
866	name: asymmetric-split-hero
867	category: hero
868	dial_compatibility:
869	  variance: [6, 10]
870	  motion: [3, 10]
871	  density: [2, 5]
872	when_to_use: "Landing pages with one strong asset and one strong message. Default hero for SaaS, agency, premium consumer."
873	not_for: "Editorial / manifesto launches where the message IS the design."
874	stack: ["react", "next", "tailwind", "motion"]
875	---
876	```
877	
878	### 12.C Required Body Sections
879	1. **Visual sketch** - short ASCII or description of the layout.
880	2. **Props API** - the component's interface.
881	3. **Code sketch** - minimal working implementation (Server Component default, Client island for motion).
882	4. **Mobile fallback** - explicit collapse rules for `< 768px`.
883	5. **Motion variants** - one variant per `MOTION_INTENSITY` band (1-3, 4-7, 8-10). Reduced-motion fallback explicit.
884	6. **Dark-mode notes** - token strategy specific to this block.
885	7. **Anti-patterns** - common ways this block goes wrong.
886	8. **References** - links to real examples in production.
887	
888	### 12.D Block-Library Discipline
889	* One block per file. No multi-block files.
890	* Every block must work standalone (drop it into a page, it renders).
891	* Every block must pass the Pre-Flight Check (Section 14).
892	* Blocks that depend on a design system from Section 2.A live under `blocks/<category>/<name>--<system>.md` (e.g. `feature/bento-grid--material.md`).
893	
894	---
895	
896	## 13. OUT OF SCOPE
897	
898	This skill is NOT for:
899	* Dashboards / dense product UI / admin panels (use Fluent, Carbon, Atlassian, or Polaris from Section 2.A).
900	* Data tables (use TanStack Table or AG Grid).
901	* Multi-step forms / wizards (use Form-specific patterns; this skill won't make them better).
902	* Code editors (use Monaco / CodeMirror with their official skinning).
903	* Native mobile (use Apple HIG / Material directly).
904	* Realtime collab UIs (presence, cursors, OT-aware - different problem class).
905	
906	If the brief is one of the above, **say so explicitly**, point to the right tool, and only apply this skill's marketing-page / about-page / landing-page parts to the surfaces where they apply.
907	
908	---
909	
910	## 14. FINAL PRE-FLIGHT CHECK
911	
912	Run this matrix before outputting code. This is the last filter.
913	
914	**THIS IS NOT OPTIONAL. Run every box. If any box fails, the output is not done.**
915	
916	- [ ] **Brief inference** declared (Section 0.B one-liner)?
917	- [ ] **Dial values** explicit and reasoned from the brief, not silently using baseline?
918	- [ ] **Design system** chosen from Section 2 if applicable, or aesthetic labeled honestly?
919	- [ ] **Redesign mode** detected and audit performed (if applicable, Section 11)?
920	- [ ] **ZERO em-dashes (`—`) anywhere on the page.** Headlines, eyebrows, pills, body, quotes, attribution, captions, buttons, alt text. Zero. (Section 9.G - non-negotiable.)
921	- [ ] **Page Theme Lock**: ONE theme (light, dark, or auto) for the whole page. No section flips to inverted mode mid-page (Section 4.11)?
922	- [ ] **Color Consistency Lock**: one accent color used identically across all sections (Section 4.2)?
923	- [ ] **Shape Consistency Lock**: one corner-radius system applied consistently (Section 4.4)?
924	- [ ] **Button Contrast Check**: every CTA text is readable against its background (no white-on-white, WCAG AA 4.5:1)?
925	- [ ] **CTA Button Wrap**: no CTA label wraps to 2+ lines at desktop?
926	- [ ] **Form Contrast Check**: form inputs, placeholders, focus rings, labels all pass WCAG AA against the section background?
927	- [ ] **Serif discipline**: if a serif is used, it is NOT Fraunces or Instrument_Serif (or it is, with explicit brand justification)? Different serif from your previous project?
928	- [ ] **Premium-consumer palette check**: if the brief is premium-consumer (cookware / wellness / artisan / luxury), the palette is NOT the AI-default beige+brass+oxblood+espresso family? Different family from your previous premium-consumer project?
929	- [ ] **Italic descender clearance**: every italic word with `y g j p q` has `leading-[1.1]` min + `pb-1` reserve?
930	- [ ] **Hero fits the viewport**: headline ≤ 2 lines, subtext ≤ 20 words AND ≤ 4 lines, CTA visible without scroll, font scale planned around image?
931	- [ ] **Hero top padding**: max `pt-24` at desktop, hero content does not float halfway down the viewport?
932	- [ ] **Hero stack discipline**: max 4 text elements in hero (eyebrow OR brand strip, headline, subtext, CTAs)? No tiny tagline below CTAs, no trust micro-strip in hero?
933	- [ ] **EYEBROW COUNT (mechanical)**: count instances of `uppercase tracking` micro-labels above section headlines across all components. Count ≤ ceil(sectionCount / 3)? Hero counts as 1.
934	- [ ] **Split-Header Ban**: no "left big headline + right small explainer paragraph" pattern as a section header (vertical stack instead)?
935	- [ ] **Zigzag Alternation Cap**: no 3+ consecutive sections with the same image+text-split layout?
936	- [ ] **No Duplicate CTA Intent**: no two CTAs with the same intent ("Get in touch" + "Let's talk" both on page = Fail)?
937	- [ ] **Logo wall = logo only**: no industry / category labels printed below logos?
938	- [ ] **Bento Background Diversity**: at least 2-3 bento cells have real visual variation (image, gradient, pattern), not all white-on-white text cards?
939	- [ ] **"Used by / Trusted by" logo wall** lives UNDER the hero, not inside it, uses REAL SVG logos (Simple Icons / devicon) or generated SVG marks, NOT plain text wordmarks?
940	- [ ] **Copy Self-Audit**: every visible string re-read, no grammatically-broken or AI-hallucinated phrases ("free on its past" type) shipped?
941	- [ ] **Motion motivated**: every animation can be justified in one sentence (hierarchy / storytelling / feedback / state transition), no GSAP-for-show?
942	- [ ] **Marquee max-one-per-page**: no two horizontal marquees on the same page?
943	- [ ] **Navigation on ONE line** at desktop, height ≤ 80px?
944	- [ ] **Section-Layout-Repetition** check: no two sections share the same layout family (at least 4 different families across 8 sections)?
945	- [ ] **Bento has rhythm AND exact cell count** (N items → N cells, no empty cells in middle or at end)?
946	- [ ] **Long lists use the right UI component** (not default `<ul>` with `divide-y` for > 5 items - see Section 4.9 alternatives)?
947	- [ ] **Real images used** (gen-tool first, then Picsum-seed, then explicit placeholder slots) - NO div-based fake screenshots, NO hand-rolled decorative SVGs, NO pure-text minimalism?
948	- [ ] **No pills/labels overlaid on images** (no `Plate · Brand`, no `Field notes - journal`)?
949	- [ ] **No photo-credit captions as decoration** (`Field study no. 12 · Ines Caetano`)?
950	- [ ] **No version footers** (`v1.4.2`, `Build 0048`) on marketing pages?
951	- [ ] **No micro-meta-sentences** under eyebrows ("Each of these is a feature we ship today...")?
952	- [ ] **No decoration text strip at hero bottom** (`BRAND. MOTION. SPATIAL.`)?
953	- [ ] **No floating top-right sub-text** in section headings?
954	- [ ] **No scoring/progress bars with filled background tracks** as comparison visuals?
955	- [ ] **No locale / city-name / time / weather strips** unless brief is genuinely globally-distributed or place-focused?
956	- [ ] **No scroll cues** (`Scroll`, `↓ scroll`, `Scroll to explore`)?
957	- [ ] **No version labels in hero** (V0.6, BETA, INVITE-ONLY) unless the brief is a launch?
958	- [ ] **No section-numbering eyebrows** (`00 / INDEX`, `001 · Capabilities`, `06 · how it works`)?
959	- [ ] **No decorative dots** (zero by default, only for real semantic state)?
960	- [ ] **No `border-t` + `border-b` on every row** of long lists / spec tables?
961	- [ ] **Content density** sane: no 20-row data tables, no fake-precise specs without justification, ≤ 25-word sub-paragraphs by default?
962	- [ ] **Quotes ≤ 3 lines** of body, attribution clean (no em-dash)?
963	- [ ] **Motion claimed = motion shown**: if `MOTION_INTENSITY > 4`, page actually animates, not just claimed?
964	- [ ] **GSAP sticky-stack / horizontal-pan** implemented per Section 5.A / 5.B canonical skeleton (`start: "top top"`, `pin: true`, correct scrub)?
965	- [ ] **No `window.addEventListener('scroll')`** - using Motion `useScroll()` / ScrollTrigger / IntersectionObserver / CSS scroll-driven animations only?
966	- [ ] **Reduced motion** wrapped for everything `MOTION_INTENSITY > 3`?
967	- [ ] **Dark mode** tokens defined and tested in both modes?
968	- [ ] **Mobile collapse** explicit (`w-full`, `px-4`, `max-w-7xl mx-auto`) for high-variance layouts?
969	- [ ] **Viewport stability**: `min-h-[100dvh]`, never `h-screen`?
970	- [ ] **`useEffect` animations** have strict cleanup functions?
971	- [ ] **Empty / loading / error** states provided?
972	- [ ] **Cards omitted** in favor of spacing where possible?
973	- [ ] **Icons** from an allowed library only (Phosphor / HugeIcons / Radix / Tabler), no hand-rolled SVG paths?
974	- [ ] **Motion** isolated in client-leaf components with `'use client'` at the top, memoized?
975	- [ ] **No AI Tells** from Section 9 (Inter as default, AI-purple, three-equal cards, Jane Doe, Acme, "Quietly in use at")?
976	- [ ] **Core Web Vitals** plausibly hit (LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1)?
977	- [ ] **One design system** per project (no Material + shadcn mixed)?
978	
979	If a single checkbox cannot be honestly ticked, the page is not done. Fix it before delivering.
980	
981	---
982	
983	# APPENDICES - Real Source-Backed Reference Material
984	
985	The sections below are vendored reference content. They give the agent real install commands, real canonical doc links, and real working starter snippets for each design system named in Section 2. Use them to ground decisions in production reality, not training-data fiction.
986	
987	## Appendix A - Install Commands per Design System
988	
989	```bash
990	# Material Web (Material 3)
991	npm install @material/web
992	
993	# Fluent UI React (v9)
994	npm install @fluentui/react-components
995	
996	# Fluent UI Web Components (framework-free)
997	npm install @fluentui/web-components @fluentui/tokens
998	
999	# IBM Carbon
1000	npm install @carbon/react @carbon/styles
1001	
1002	# Radix Themes
1003	npm install @radix-ui/themes
1004	
1005	# shadcn/ui (open code, owned components)
1006	npx shadcn@latest init
1007	npx shadcn@latest add button card badge separator input
1008	
1009	# Primer CSS (GitHub product/devtool UI)
1010	npm install --save @primer/css
1011	
1012	# Primer Brand (GitHub marketing UI)
1013	npm install @primer/react-brand
1014	
1015	# GOV.UK Frontend
1016	npm install govuk-frontend
1017	
1018	# USWDS (US Web Design System)
1019	npm install uswds
1020	
1021	# Atlassian Design System (Atlaskit)
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding mod browser UI and the card/grid view toggle pattern:

## Nexus Mods Implementation

Nexus Mods is exploring display options such as grid and list views for their mod browsing interface. The grid and list view was something many users preferred, offering more content visibility on the page. This addresses a key UX consideration where the new design improves the hierarchy of content, making it easier to browse across different sections on a page while standardising elements of the layout across different pages.

## CurseForge User Preference

Users have consistently requested view toggle functionality similar to CurseForge. CurseForge offers a configuration switch between tiles and list UI format, which users appreciate for browsing mods and modpacks, as this provides better visual density and aesthetics.

## General UX Design Pattern

From a broader UX perspective, a card view is a grid of cards in a gallery to facilitate browsing, and card views are typically used to present data set summaries, allowing users to drill down into any card to see more detailed content. Additionally, a card view that toggles into a table or list view is a recognized design pattern when content views need to change based on user preference.

This toggle pattern represents a common solution for balancing visual browsing (card/grid view) with content density (list view), allowing users to choose their preferred interface.

 `https://www.orbix.studio/blogs/bento-grid-dashboard-design-aesthetics`

```text
# Bento Grid Dashboard Design: Key Claims & Recommendations

**Publish Date:** May 9, 2026

## Core Claims

**Definition & Purpose:**
The article defines bento grid as "a modular UI layout where the interface divides into rectangular tiles of varying sizes, each containing one type of content." The spatial arrangement itself communicates data hierarchy without requiring additional labels.

**Why It Matters for SaaS:**
Users form dashboard impressions in under six seconds. Bento grids address "data density problem" by using Gestalt principles—proximity and similarity—to guide visual scanning. Unlike card layouts where every element "competes for the same amount of attention," bento sizing pre-answers where users should focus.

**CSS Grid Foundation:**
The guide recommends starting with "a 12-column grid" because it "gives maximum flexibility." The core code pattern uses `grid-column: span X` and `grid-row: span X` rather than Flexbox, which is "one-dimensional."

**Tile Size Hierarchy:**
- Hero tiles: 4–6 columns × 2 rows (primary KPI)
- Feature tiles: 3–4 columns × 1–2 rows (supporting context)
- Metric cards: 2–3 columns × 1 row (secondary KPIs)
- Accent tiles: 1–2 columns × 1 row (alerts, actions)

**Critical Gap:** Tile size must "reflect data importance, not data volume." Oversized tiles for minor metrics waste space; undersized tiles for key metrics create hierarchy confusion.

## Accessibility Requirements (Frequently Missed)

The article emphasizes "the gap most designers skip"—WCAG 2.1 AA compliance requires:
- DOM order matching visual order (CSS Grid can separate these)
- ARIA labels on every tile container
- 4.5:1 color contrast minimum
- Visible `:focus-visible` rings for keyboard navigation

Dark mode implementations commonly fail contrast ratios, particularly with glassmorphism effects.

## Common Design Mistakes

1. **Equal tile sizes** = card layout, not bento grid
2. **Three+ hero tiles** eliminate visual anchoring
3. **No responsive collapse plan** for mobile
4. **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** from asynchronous tile loading (penalties: -10–15 Lighthouse points)
5. **Underestimating whitespace role:** Recommend 16px gap; below 8px tiles merge; above 32px loses cohesion

## Real-World Examples

- **Apple:** Uses bento on product pages; "spatial weight communicates feature priority"
- **Datadog:** Most "technically mature bento implementation"; users resize across 12-column grid with snap-to-grid
- **Linear:** Dashboard uses "visual progress indicators" in large tiles; smaller metric cards signal secondary data
- **Payhawk:** Users "understand company spending health in under four seconds"

## Direct Quote (Under 125 Characters)

"A bento grid dashboard is not a visual style. It is an information architecture decision."

---

**Key Takeaway:** Bento grid succeeds when tile sizing reflects data hierarchy *before* visual design. Accessibility and responsive behavior require upfront planning rather than afterthought implementation.
```
</details></tool-use>

# Bento Grid CSS Layout Claims from shadcn.io

**Publication Date:** Last updated April 8, 2026

## Specific Technical Claims

**Grid Structure:**
The component features "a six-cell bento layout using varied col-span and row-span sizes to create visual weight and hierarchy." This indicates asymmetric sizing where cells occupy different numbers of columns and rows.

**Layout Asymmetry:**
Described as "an asymmetric bento grid hero" with "crosshair image placeholders in varied col-span cells," establishing that column-spanning varies across the grid to create visual hierarchy.

**Responsive Design:**
The block "adapt[s] to mobile screens," though specific breakpoint details aren't provided in the excerpt.

## Design Patterns

**Visual Hierarchy:**
The varied cell sizes deliberately create "visual weight and hierarchy," suggesting larger cells emphasize featured content while smaller cells support secondary information.

**Content Signaling:**
"Each cell includes a small label overlay so designers know what content to place," indicating a template system with predetermined positions for product screenshots or feature visuals.

**Use Cases Mentioned:**
Suited for "multi-feature SaaS platforms, design tool landing pages, developer dashboards, and any product with several distinct views."

## Implementation Details

Built with "Tailwind CSS grid utilities," "shadcn/ui," TypeScript, and "motion/react staggered entrance animations."

<tool-use data-tool-type="read" data-tool-name="WebFetch"><details>
<summary>Tool use: **WebFetch**</summary>
 `https://www.superdesign.dev/styles/bento-grid`

```text
# Bento Grid CSS Technical Claims

## Core CSS Architecture

**Grid Structure:**
- "display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(4, minmax(0, 1fr))" creates four equal tracks
- "minmax(0, 1fr) instead of bare 1fr prevents long unbroken content (URLs, code) from blowing out a track"
- "grid-auto-rows: 180px" establishes fixed row heights for predictable spanning behavior

**Cell Styling:**
- Border radius between 20–28px is essential; the document notes "under 16px it reads as a plain card grid"
- "padding: 24px" and "overflow: hidden" clip edge-to-edge media to rounded corners
- Single consistent gap (16px) across all cells sells the tray aesthetic

**Spacing & Layout:**
- "gap: 16px" maintains uniform gutters throughout
- "grid-auto-flow: dense" fills holes left by spanning cells but "decouples visual order from DOM order"

## Hierarchy & Spans

- "exactly one 2x2 anchor cell creates the hierarchy" via "grid-column: span 2; grid-row: span 2"
- Secondary cells use "grid-column: span 2" for wide layouts or single 1×1 spans for dense backfill

## Responsive Behavior

The document prescribes breakpoint resets:
- At 768px: "grid-template-columns: repeat(2, minmax(0, 1fr))"
- At 480px: "grid-template-columns: 1fr; grid-auto-rows: auto" with spans reset to "grid-column: auto; grid-row: auto"
- "Relax the fixed row unit on one-column layouts" to prevent silent clipping on mobile

## Accessibility Limitations

- "grid-auto-flow: dense and span juggling reorder cells visually while screen readers and keyboard focus follow DOM order," violating WCAG 2.4.3
- Text over imagery requires "4.5:1 check" per cell; a "bottom scrim, rgba(0,0,0,0.55) to transparent" fixes contrast failures

## When Not to Use

- Mobile-heavy traffic: "A 4-column bento collapses to 8 to 12 stacked cards, which is just a long, hierarchy-free scroll"
- Long-form content: "Cells force content down to a phrase, a number, or one screenshot"
- Equal-sized cells: "If every cell is the same size you have a plain card grid wearing a trendy name"

## Browser Support

"CSS Grid and grid-auto-flow: dense have shipped in every major browser since 2017" with no prefixes needed in 2026.
```

 `https://effect-labs.com/en/pages/blog/bento-grid-layouts.html`

```text
# Concrete Claims from "Bento Grid Layouts: The Complete CSS Guide"

**Publication Date:** February 2, 2026

## CSS Grid Properties & Techniques

**Grid Structure Basics:**
- The article states: "Grid allows controlling rows AND columns simultaneously, offering unmatched compositional freedom" compared to Flexbox.
- `grid-column: span 2` and `grid-row: span 2` enable elements to occupy multiple cells.
- Placement syntax `1 / 3` means "element starts at line 1 and ends at line 3."
- `1 / -1` spans "from the first to the last line."

**grid-template-areas Method:**
- Described as offering "a visual and intuitive syntax" for complex layouts.
- The article claims this approach makes layouts "readable at a glance" and facilitates "maintenance and modifications."

## Card Size Strategies

Three distinct card types are detailed:
- **Feature cards** span 2 columns × 2 rows for hero content
- **Wide cards** span 2 columns for horizontal messaging
- **Tall cards** span 2 rows for vertical content

## Animation Performance

Direct claim: "Prioritize `transform` and `opacity` for your animations. These properties are GPU-optimized and do not trigger layout recalculation."

## Responsive Design Approach

The guide recommends reducing columns from 4 (desktop) → 2 (tablet) → 1 (mobile) with corresponding layout restructuring.

## Accessibility Requirements

Focus states require: "2px solid outline with 2px outline-offset" for keyboard navigation.

Motion preferences: `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` should disable animations entirely.
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding large catalog card grid UX design with focus on download counts, category badges, placement, and scannability in ecommerce and app store contexts:

## Key Findings on Card Grid UX

**Hierarchy & Information Placement:**
Amazon prioritizes hierarchy effectively: image dominates, price sits right below, Prime badge and star rating give instant trust signals. Pricing and key status should be placed near the image for shopping and catalog browsing contexts.

**Scannability Strategies:**
Badges and compact stats are used for fast scanning, and card UI designs can present small snippets of information, letting users quickly skim through and identify what matters most to them. However, card layouts are less scannable than lists because the positioning of individual elements is less predictable for the eye.

**Product Card Best Practices:**
Major e-commerce sites like Amazon, Alibaba, Walmart, and eBay use card components with product image, title, price, discount, and a call-to-action. A high-converting product listing page needs visible star ratings and review counts on every card, clear pricing with sale prices crossed out, an "Add to Cart" button and a distinct "Quick View" option.

**Avoiding Visual Density:**
Card grids can become visually dense or noisy when too much state is shown at once.

# CSS Grid Masonry Layout: Technical Claims Extract

**Publish Date:** March 31, 2026 (posted on DEV Community)

## Core Technical Claims

**Syntax & Implementation:**
- "CSS Grid Lanes adds a new display mode that creates a masonry-style layout using the familiar grid syntax."
- Primary syntax: `grid-template-rows: masonry;` triggers masonry packing on the row axis
- Alternative future syntax: `display: grid-lanes;`

**Browser Support (as of early 2026):**
- "Safari 26 just shipped it first" with native masonry support
- Chrome and Firefox support behind experimental flags, "expected to ship stable support later in 2026"

**Key Functional Claims:**
- Items flow into the axis with the most available space, creating "tightly packed layout without the ugly gaps"
- "The browser handles all the packing logic natively" (no JavaScript required)
- Preserves DOM order for "accessibility and keyboard navigation," unlike the older CSS column approach

**Accessibility Advantage:**
- "CSS Grid Lanes preserves DOM order," contrasting with the column hack where "items are ordered vertically...rather than naturally"
- The column method "breaks accessibility because the DOM order doesn't match visual order"

**Progressive Enhancement Pattern:**
- Recommended fallback: regular grid with `align-items: start` for unsupported browsers
- Implementation via `@supports (grid-template-rows: masonry)` feature queries

## Notable Omissions
No performance metrics, rendering benchmarks, or specific browser version timelines beyond Safari 26 are provided.

# Concrete Claims from "CSS Layout Patterns in 2026"

**Published:** March 11, 2026 (Updated May 19, 2026)

## Grid vs Flexbox Claims

**Author's audit finding:**
"when I audited one client's codebase, they had 340 uses of `display: flex` and 12 uses of `display: grid`. After refactoring, those numbers flipped to roughly 180 flex and 90 grid, and the total CSS shrank by 23%."

**Use case guidance:**
- Grid: "page-level structure, card grids, dashboards, bento grids"
- Flexbox: "component-level alignment, navigation"

**Mobile failure pattern:**

## Subgrid Browser Support

**Global adoption:** "counting older installs still sits below 90% on caniuse"

## Accessibility Issue

**Grid reordering problem:** An audited bento layout had "featured card was placed first visually... but was the seventh element in the DOM. Tab order jumped from top-left to bottom-right and back."

**Solution available:** The `reading-flow` property ships in "Chrome 131+" to align visual and navigation order.

```text
# CSS Masonry Layout: Technical Claims and Specifications

**Publication Date:** October 21, 2024

## Concrete Technical Claims

### Specification Status
- **Current proposal structure:** "Currently, the specification is drafted with two options, going head-to-head — the 'Grid Integrated' option and the 'Grid Independent' option."

- **Working Draft published:** The article states the CSSWG "published a First Public Working Draft of CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3" in September 2024.

### "Just Use Grid" Syntax Option
The article describes this approach as using existing Grid properties with a new value:

```css
.masonry {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-rows: masonry; /* final name TBD */
  gap: 10px;
}
```

Key claim: "The Just Use Grid option adds _only_ _one new value_ to CSS" (the `masonry`/`collapse` value for track definitions).

### "New Masonry Layout" Syntax Option
Competing approach with dedicated properties:

```css
.masonry {
  display: masonry;
  masonry-template-tracks: repeat(3, 1fr);
  gap: 10px;
}
```

### Browser Implementation Status
- **Safari:** Available in Safari Technology Preview and Safari 17.x/18.x with feature flag enabled
- **Firefox:** Supported in Firefox 77+ with flag enabled
- **Chrome:** Team has published position against unified Grid syntax

### Performance Concerns Resolution
"Over the last six months, the key performance concerns have been addressed. Yes, it is possible to integrate Masonry layout with the full power of CSS Grid."

### Key Difference: Property Redundancy
The article documents that the "New Masonry Layout" option creates ~10 duplicate properties mapping Grid concepts to masonry equivalents (e.g., `grid-column` → `masonry-track`, `grid-template-columns` → `masonry-template-tracks`).

### Proposed Advanced Feature
Both options include a new entirely-new property: a "slack" mechanism for determining item placement tolerance, allowing browsers to ignore negligible position differences.

### Unsettled Naming Issue
The article notes: "masonry is not an ideal name" and proposes alternatives `collapse` or `pack` as values instead, with community feedback still being gathered.
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# Masonry in CSS: Concrete Claims Extract

**Article Publication Date:** May 6, 2025

## CSS Grid Masonry Proposal

**Syntax:**
```css
grid-template-rows: masonry
```

**Status:** "experimental in Firefox Nightly" only. The article states this is part of "CSS Grid Level 3" draft specification.

**How it works:** Items "flow vertically, respecting column tracks but not row constraints" while columns remain as grid axes.

**Browser Support:** Limited to Firefox Nightly with flag enabled. The article explicitly notes: "it's not the seamless solution you might expect, thanks to limited browser support."

## Key Criticism Points

Rachel Andrew argues masonry's organic flow fundamentally "contrasts with Grid's strict two-dimensional structure." Ahmad Shadeed contends it "makes the grid layout more complex than it should be."

Geoff Graham emphasizes: "teaching and learning grid to get to understand masonry behaviour unnecessarily lumps two different formatting contexts into one."

## Current Workarounds

**Grid hack approach:** Uses `grid-auto-rows: 10px` with JavaScript calculating `gridRowEnd` spans based on measured item heights. The article notes this "relies on JavaScript to calculate spans, defeating the 'no JavaScript' dream."

**Problems identified:**
- Requires recalculation on resize/content changes
- Logical DOM order may not match visual flow
- Lazy-loaded content causes layout jumps
- Creates "mental juggling act" switching between CSS and JavaScript

## Alternative: Item Flow (WebKit, March 2025)

**Four core properties:**
- `item-direction` (flow direction)
- `item-wrap` (wrapping behavior)
- `item-pack` (packing density: sparse/dense/balance)
- `item-slack` (spacing tolerance)

**Example syntax:**
```css
item-flow: column wrap dense;
```

**Status:** "not yet implemented in browsers as of April 2025" and "still in discussion."

**Limitation:** Naming debates ongoing; "properties like `item-slack` face naming debates due to clarity issues for non-native English speakers."

## Performance Characteristics

The JavaScript grid hack "can lead to lag on complex pages" when recalculating spans, per Chris Coyier's critique.
```
</details></tool-use>

## Design Specifications

**Card Size Hierarchy:**
- 1×1 box (100px × 100px): Supporting features, secondary actions
- 2×1 box (216px × 100px): Mid-tier features with horizontal emphasis
- 2×2 box (216px × 216px): Hero feature, primary value proposition
- 3×2 box (332px × 216px): Detailed feature demonstrations

**Standard Gutter Sizes:**
- Desktop: 16-24px
- Tablet: 12-16px
- Mobile: 12px

**Card Internal Structure:**
- Visual element: 60-70% of card height
- Headline: 20-30% of card height (1-6 words maximum)
- Supporting text: 10-20% (1-2 lines maximum)

**Responsive Breakpoints:**
- Desktop (1200px+): 3-4 columns
- Tablet (768-1199px): 2 columns
- Mobile (<768px): 1 column

**Interaction Timing:**
- Hover transitions: 200-300ms ease-out
- Active state scale: 0.98× for 100ms duration

 `https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Grid_layout/Masonry_layout`

```text
# Concrete Technical Claims About CSS Masonry Layout

## Browser Support Status

**Limited availability - Experimental feature:**
- "This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers."
- "This is an experimental technology"
- "Check the Browser compatibility table carefully before using this in production."

## Syntax and Property Names

**Display values:**
- `display: grid-lanes` (most common use case)
- `display: inline-grid-lanes`

**Required property:**
- Must be used with [`grid-template-columns`](/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/Properties/grid-template-columns) or [`grid-template-rows`](/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/Properties/grid-template-rows)

**Example:**
```css
.grid {
  display: grid-lanes;
  gap: 10px;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(120px, 1fr));
}
```

## How Masonry Differs from Regular Grid

**Core difference:**
"Masonry layout is a layout method where one axis uses a typical strict grid layout, most often columns, and the other a **stacking** (masonry) layout."

"On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps."

## Grid Axis Controls

**Spanning items work as expected:**
- Items can span multiple tracks using the `span` keyword
- Items with definite placement are positioned before masonry layout occurs

**Example with spanning:**
```css
.span-2 {
  grid-column-end: span 2;
}
```

**Line-based positioning supported:**
```css
.positioned {
  grid-column: 2 / 4;
}
```

## Fallback Behavior

"In browsers that do not support masonry, regular grid auto-placement will be used instead."

## Related Properties

- [`grid-auto-flow`](/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/Properties/grid-auto-flow) - for controlling grid auto-placement

## Specification Reference

"Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as **grid-lanes** layout)"

Source: [CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3 - grid-lanes-model](https://drafts.csswg.org/css-grid-3/#grid-lanes-model)
```
</details></tool-use>

```text
# CSS Masonry Grid: Technical Claims & Implementation Details

## Publication Date
**28 September 2021**

## Browser Support & Implementation Status

**Current (2021):** Firefox Nightly only, requiring the `layout.css.grid-template-masonry-value.enabled` flag enabled via `about:config`.

**Update Notice:** The article includes a May 2023 warning that the feature remains experimental. Readers are directed to:
- Chrome's proposal
- Webkit's proposal

The author notes: *"This article, dated 2021, presents information from its experimental phase."*

## Core CSS Syntax

The basic implementation uses:
```css
.grid {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-rows: masonry;
}
```

## Key Technical Claims

1. **No JavaScript required** for basic masonry layouts when browser support exists
2. **Grid ordering works responsively:** The `order` property successfully reorders grid items across viewports
3. **Column spanning supported:** Items can span multiple columns, though *"for some reason I wanted the grid to nicely close the items together"* — suggesting potential layout gaps when combining spans with masonry
4. **Feature detection available:** Using `@supports (grid-template-rows: masonry)` or `CSS.supports("grid-template-rows", "masonry")`

## Limitations & Concerns

- **Accessibility risks** with reordering
- **Browser inconsistencies expected** during rollout (author specifically mentions Safari concerns)
- **Limited practical adoption** noted: *"the huge hype isn't what it used to be anymore"*
```

I found relevant Nielsen Norman Group resources about card components, grid vs. list layouts, and information hierarchy. Here are the key findings:

## Card View vs. List View Comparison

List view allows for easy sorting and is space efficient, while card view is visually engaging and creates effective groupings.

## Information Hierarchy in Card Layouts

Card layouts typically deemphasize the ranking of content. However, even though they lack the strong hierarchy signals of list views, multicard displays still obey typical page-hierarchy laws: content at the top and on the left of the page is more discoverable and perceived as more important.

## Scannability Differences

Card layouts are less scannable than lists. A standard vertical list view (in which each item occupies a new row in the layout, and may still contain mixed media such as an image, title, summary, and so forth) is more scannable than cards because the positioning of the individual elements is fixed in size and more predictable for the eye.

## Best Use Cases for Cards

Cards are a useful UI component for grouping several related pieces of information together for providing a linked entry point to further details on that content. Cards work especially well in contexts where they provide summaries of many different kinds of content, rather than when simply used as a modern-looking replacement for a list of content.

## Space and Content Considerations

Cards take more space. Because cards are bigger than a line of text, any given screen size can't show as many cards in a single view as would be possible in a list view.

The Nielsen Norman Group recommends matching your layout choice to your users' needs rather than defaulting to cards solely for aesthetic reasons.

I've found relevant information about Nexus Mods' mod browsing overhaul. Here's a summary of the key findings:

## Mod Browsing Overhaul Overview

The redesign has been a significant piece of work, representing a big step forward for the site that updated fundamental parts of the website and content browsing experience that had been untouched for almost a decade.

## Design Goals

The new design improves the hierarchy of content, making it easier to browse across different sections on a page while standardising elements of the layout across different pages. Making the site easier to browse is intended to encourage more people to find and use mods, ultimately benefiting the whole community.

## Community Feedback

The response from the community has been mixed. The forum feedback thread on these changes has been viewed 280k times with 2k comments, and every comment has been read by the product, design and community teams, with most of them being not positive.

## Key Concerns & Responses

More experienced site users reported that the reduction in visible content feels like a significant loss, as grid and list views they preferred offered more content visibility, and the team is exploring additional tile options including a list view and a more compact view.

## Performance

Some pages have suffered performance issues requiring fine-tuning, though some pages are now on par with the old versions while others show improved performance.

 `https://griffadev.medium.com/building-a-responsive-progressively-enhanced-masonry-layout-with-only-css-and-html-998b2de89cf2`

```text
# Technical Claims About CSS Masonry Layouts

**Publication Date:** January 13, 2021

## CSS Grid Masonry Implementation

**Property:** `grid-template-rows: masonry`
- Quote: "Masonry layout using `grid-template-rows: masonry`"
- Status at publication: Experimental, available only in Firefox behind a feature flag
- The article notes this specification may change before broader browser adoption

**Additional Property:** `align-tracks: stretch`
- Purpose: Resolves the "wonky bottom" edge issue in masonry layouts
- Quote: "The masonry specification also allows for a new property: `align-tracks`, which you can set to `stretch`"
- Caveat mentioned: Potential issues exist when bricks span multiple columns

## CSS Columns Fallback

**Properties Used:**
- `column-width`: Sets minimum column width before shrinking
- Quote: "Setting a `column-width` will set a minimum width of a column before columns need to shrink down"
- Enables responsive behavior without media queries

**Key Difference from Grid:**
- Quote: "The order of items goes down the columns as opposed to across the rows with css grid. Columns: ⬇️ Grid: ➡️"

## Progressive Enhancement Strategy

**Method:** `@supports` CSS rule
- Enables automatic browser-based feature detection and fallback application without HTML changes
```

Based on the search results, here are key insights about app store card UI design with a focus on primary action CTA positioning at the bottom of cards:

## Primary Action Position in Card-Based Layouts

Product cards typically have straightforward anatomy including image, title, price, rating, and CTA. For action button placement specifically, action buttons such as "Add to Cart" or "Buy Now" are often arranged in a row at the bottom of each card for better alignment and accessibility.

## Visual Hierarchy and Action Organization

The card itself is usually the primary click target, with secondary actions like share or bookmark grouped consistently at the bottom, and an overflow menu used if more actions are needed. This approach prevents visual clutter across large card grids.

## Design Principles for Multiple Cards

When displaying 300+ items in a grid, consistency is crucial. When actions live in unpredictable locations, people hesitate, and that hesitation compounds across a grid of 20 cards into genuine frustration. The research emphasizes maintaining consistent action placement and visual weight across all cards to prevent user confusion.

## Key Best Practices

Your title should be noticeably larger than your description, and your CTA should be visually distinct from everything else on the card. Secondary actions like share or bookmark should be visually quieter and limited to two at most.

The search results primarily focus on general card UI patterns and CTA positioning rather than app store-specific designs. For more detailed information on download stats display and category tags in app store contexts, you may want to conduct additional research specific to app store design patterns.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about CSS grid card layouts with view toggling patterns. Here's what the web search revealed:

## Key Findings

**Card Layout Patterns with Toggle Views:**

A card view is a grid of cards in a gallery to facilitate browsing, typically used to present data set summaries, allowing users to drill down into any card to see more detailed content. Card views can toggle into a table or list view, which is a common UX pattern for managing different density levels.

**Grid Implementation Techniques:**

Dynamic grid-spacing configurations are engineered natively using CSS Grid with repeat(auto-fit, minmax()) structures, preventing visual overlap without breaking column flow.

**React Toggle Implementation:**

The process involves changing the layout view from grid to list and back using conditional rendering in React, a few lines of custom CSS and Bootstrap classes. A boolean can toggle between the grid and list view, where the default value (true) means it's in grid view, and if it's false, it will be in list view.

**Modern Card Design (2025):**

A modern CSS card design for websites 2025 combines clarity, interactivity, and responsiveness in one elegant package.

**Open-Source Resources:**

Uiverse's library provides open-source, responsive cards for web and mobile applications, browsable from minimalistic to feature-rich styles. Additionally, PatternFly provides card view design guidelines as an open-source design system resource.

```text
# Density Toggle Claims from Material React Table V3 Docs

## Core Functionality
Material React Table includes a built-in density toggle feature that "lets you toggle between three different density levels" for accessibility and user preferences.

## Density States
The library supports three distinct density options:
- **Spacious** - maximum whitespace
- **Comfortable** - default setting, medium density
- **Compact** - minimal height, with whitespace set to `nowrap` by default

## Default Behavior
"By default, Material React Table will render with a medium `comfortable` density." A toggle appears in the top toolbar allowing users to cycle through all three options.

## Configuration Claims
- Density toggling is enabled by default via the `enableDensityToggle: true` prop
- Default can be changed through `initialState: { density: 'compact' }` or the `state` option
- The toggle can be disabled entirely while maintaining a specific density setting

## UX Guidance
The documentation notes that "When a `compact` density is set, whitespace is set to `nowrap` by default to keep the rows as short in height as possible," though this can be overridden through styling props. This represents an intentional information hierarchy optimization for space-constrained views.
```
</details></tool-use>

 `https://cloudscape.design/foundation/visual-foundation/content-density/`

```text
# Content Density Claims from Cloudscape Design System

## Core Definitions

**Content Density Definition:**
"Content density is defined by the ratio of information visible compared to the space available in the interface."

## Density Modes

**Two supported modes:**
- "Comfortable is the standard density level of the system, active by default."
- "Compact is an additional density level for data intensive views."

## Specific Implementation Claims

**Spacing Scale:**
The system uses a 4px base unit where "in compact mode, the spacing scale is reduced in increments of 4, decreasing the vertical spacing inside components and between components."

**Default Requirement:**
"Always set comfortable mode as default. Comfortable mode is the default density level of Cloudscape. Compact mode can hinder readability, overwhelm, and prolong content consumption."

## Scope Limitations

**Readability Exceptions:**
Compact mode is "not fully applied to informational components" like help panels, alerts, and form validation messages to maintain readability.

**Interactivity Exceptions:**
Compact mode is "not fully applied to elements with limited target space such as dropdowns" in select, multiselect, and date picker components.

## Falsifiable Claims

- Compact mode propagates "reduced spacing scale to all components, in all view types" (with noted exceptions)
- Users can toggle between modes without reloading applications
- Comfortable mode optimizes "content consumption and readability, as well as cross device experiences"
```
</details></tool-use>

 `https://www.patternfly.org/patterns/card-view/design-guidelines/`

```text
# Card View Documentation Analysis

## Concrete Claims Found

**View Mode Switchers:**
The documentation mentions toggling between views: "A card that opens an expandable panel on the right. A card view that toggles into a table or list view."

**Grid Layouts:**
The pattern uses a gallery grid approach with responsive behavior. Cards "will size down appropriately based on the built-in CSS responsiveness behaviors" and "follow the grid layout, expanding and compressing at certain breakpoints."

**Information Hierarchy:**
Cards present "data set summaries, allowing users to drill down into any card to see more detailed content." The design emphasizes visual identification: "Display items in a data set that are best identified by a graphic or other visual representation."

**Card Components:**
Required/optional elements include:
- Card body or footer for single inline actions
- Card header with top-right kebab for multiple actions
- Checkboxes in top-right corner for global actions
- Optional expandable sections with "expand all/collapse all" controls

**Grid Spacing & Backgrounds:**
Two background options exist based on use case—white backgrounds when views can change, gray backgrounds when cards are the sole content display. The documentation notes: "Use the default border treatment (cards with drop shadows) for cards when placed on a gray background only. Flat cards should be used when placing cards on a white background."

**Mobile Responsiveness:**
At smallest sizes, cards "take up almost the entire width of the screen."

**No UX Research Findings:** The documentation contains no explicit research data or studies.

**No Masonry/Bento/Featured Cards:** These layouts are not mentioned in the provided content.
```
</details></tool-use>

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
The `design-taste-frontend` skill is a 1,207-line production-grade design system codified as a skill — it has an explicit **Three Dials** system (`DESIGN_VARIANCE`, `MOTION_INTENSITY`, `VISUAL_DENSITY`) that directly controls grid density and layout choices. The Reference Vocabulary in Section 10 names every pattern we'd want for the mod browser: Bento Grid, Masonry Layout, Chroma Grid, Spotlight Border Card, Drag-to-Pan Grid.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Here's what the local skills give us directly for the mod browser layout:

---

**Three Dials for the CoX Mod Browser** (from `design-taste-frontend`):

| Dial | Recommended | Reasoning |
|------|-------------|-----------|
| `DESIGN_VARIANCE` | **8** | Superhero/CoH aesthetic = asymmetric, grid-breaking, overlaps |
| `MOTION_INTENSITY` | **6** | Hover reveals, stagger reveals on load — but not cinematic |
| `VISUAL_DENSITY` | **6** | 291 cards need density, but not cockpit-tight |

---

**Layout Patterns to apply** (Section 10 vocabulary, directly usable):

| Pattern | Where in mod browser |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Bento Grid** | Featured/top-5-by-downloads hero section — mixed tile sizes |
| **Masonry Layout** | Main card grid — variable description lengths fill naturally |
| **Chroma Grid** | Category tab filter row — subtle gradient borders animate on hover |
| **Spotlight Border Card** | Every mod card — border illuminates under cursor |
| **Drag-to-Pan Grid** | Optional: boundless canvas mode for power users |

**Hard rules from the skill that apply:**
- Bento cell count = exactly N items, no empty cells
- Bento Background Diversity: at least 2-3 cells with real visual variation (CoH zone images are perfect)
- `DESIGN_VARIANCE 8` means: `grid-template-columns: 2fr 1fr 1fr` fractional units, not equal `1fr 1fr 1fr`
- Cards only when elevation communicates hierarchy — use `border` + negative space for the list view

The deep-research workflow is still running and will add web-sourced verification on top of this. Once it lands I'll synthesize both into a concrete layout spec for `generate.py`. Want me to also read the `taste-skill` directory to see if there are block implementations (Section 12 block library) already written for bento or masonry?

### Please confirm the following.

- [x] I checked the [existing issues](https://github.com/modrinth/code/issues) for duplicate feature requests
- [x] I have checked that this feature request is not on our [roadmap](https://roadmap.modrinth.com)

### What parts of Modrinth is your feature request related too?

App

### Is your suggested feature related to a problem? Please describe.

I hate having to look and observe my modpacks through a list as well as browse and search for mods through a list. I wish there was a way to configure it just like Curseforge like how you can change it to tiles which are much easier on my eyes and also look prettier and also when browsing for mods. And is the one feature Modrinth is lacking before I can fully commit to using the app and ditching Curseforge for good.

### Describe the solution you'd like

My solution is having a configuration switch between tiles and list ui format just like Curseforge. For both when you're in your project and when you're browsing for mods in the app. Preferably maybe there could also be a option as well for boxy icons and squares with rounded corners. Here I've provided some mock-up images and also examples from Curseforge to show what I mean

![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/10e20379-4651-425b-898b-b6769ed77b73)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/81bca742-cd31-4895-be83-cf0748be1f1c)
![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/24b3a1ff-a0b6-4214-8fce-41b031fd7cda)



### Describe alternatives you've considered

I don't really have any other alternate solutions to this problem other than making the UI not feel like you're doom scroll or having options for how you can navigate the app.

### Additional context

I really like your app however having a ui that doesn't really show much on your screen and have the mods take up half of screen space is isn't really efficient. By having a way to choose what ui suits people the best like Curseforge will improve the experience of the app drastically.
```
