Based on the search results, I found the WebKit blog post you're looking for. Here's what I found:

The CSS Masonry specification is drafted with two options, going head-to-head — the "Grid Integrated" option and the "Grid Independent" option.

**Key details about the two options:**

1. **Grid Integrated Option**: This option integrates masonry directly into CSS Grid. Apple believes "Masonry" should be part of CSS Grid, as this functionality expands CSS Grid to create columnar grids and should be mixed with all the other features of Grid, including options for defining columns, track spanning, explicit placement, and subgrid.

2. **Grid Independent Option**: The Chrome team believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed. Rachel Andrew argued in favor of the Grid Independent option, noting that good defaults make things easier to teach and mean less configuration, and developers won't have to write as much code if this feature becomes its own display type with new default values suited for Masonry layouts.

The WebKit blog post about this was published in April 2024, with demos available at webkit.org/demos/grid3.

Based on the search results, here's what the WebKit CSS masonry syntax proposal and controversy is about:

## The Core Debate

A mechanism in CSS for "masonry layout" was first proposed by Mozilla in January 2020 as an extension of CSS Grid, and implemented as an experiment behind a flag in Firefox Nightly. In 2022, Apple started implementing this CSS Grid Level 3 proposal in Safari Technology Preview, which sparked significant controversy.

## The Two Main Proposals

The "Just Use Grid" option leans into the idea that CSS Grid is a major layout mechanism for web pages and should keep expanding to be more and more powerful. The "New Masonry Layout" option seems to say that Grid should stay as Grid, and add new segregated display types each time we add more layout capabilities.

In practical terms, WebKit supports the "Just Use Grid" approach, proposing syntax like `grid-template-rows: masonry`. Meanwhile, the Chrome team believes that masonry should be a separate layout method, defined using display: masonry (or another keyword should a better name be decided upon).

## Key Technical Arguments

Grid and masonry are opposite in terms of how the browser deals with sizing and placement. When a grid is laid out, all items are placed before layout and the browser knows exactly what is in each track. This enables the complex intrinsic sizing that's so useful in grid. With masonry, the items are placed as they are laid out, and the browser doesn't know how many are in each track. This isn't a problem with all intrinsic sized tracks or all fixed sized tracks but is if you mix fixed and intrinsic tracks. To get round the problem, the browser needs to do a pre-layout step of laying out every item in every possible way to get measurements, with a large grid this would contribute to layout performance issues.

## Recent Developments

Masonry will be a new display type, but must include the word "grid" in the name. The exact keyword is still being debated. The CSSWG has resolved to proceed with the proposed item-flow approach.

# "Just Use Grid" Option Analysis

**Article Date:** October 21, 2024

## Core Syntax Addition

The "Just Use Grid" option does **not** require a new display type. Instead, it adds a single new value to existing properties:

> "The Just Use Grid option adds _only_ _one new value_ to CSS. You can create masonry-style layouts by simply using the `collapse` value in a track definition"

The new syntax uses existing grid properties with this additional value:

```css
grid-template-rows: collapse  /* or grid-template-columns: collapse */
```

## Example Comparison

The article provides this direct contrast. Using the "Just Use Grid" approach for a 3-column masonry layout:

> ```
> .masonry {
>   display: grid;
>   grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
>   grid-template-rows: masonry; /* final name TBD */
>   gap: 10px;
> }
> ```

## Key Design Philosophy

Rather than introducing redundant properties, the approach leverages existing CSS Grid infrastructure:

> "By not repurposing what's already there, New Masonry Layout creates unnecessary redundancy in CSS."

This maintains consistency with CSS design principles emphasizing minimal redundancy and reuse of established patterns.
```

## CSS Masonry Layout Debate: Key Findings

Based on recent discussions in 2024-2025, here's what the CSS masonry layout debate reveals:

### Two Main Competing Proposals

There are two competing proposals for CSS masonry at the time of writing and many opinions are flying around advocating one or the other.

1. **Grid-integrated approach**: Mozilla and WebKit have favored the grid-template-rows: masonry extension of existing grid, which is what Firefox and Safari have shipped behind flags.

2. **Display property approach**: The Chrome team still believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed. The display: masonry version creates new opportunities and a simpler syntax.

### Recent Resolution (January 2025)

Good news emerged recently: It was resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout. The CSS Working Group just discussed [css-grid-3][masonry] Masonry Syntax Debate, and agreed to the following: RESOLVED: Re-use grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout.

Additionally, it's going to be display: grid-lanes.

### Competing Perspectives

Both proposals have merits and come with potential challenges — it's a matter of what you prioritize which, in this case, I believe is a choice between leveraging existing CSS layout features and the ergonomics of a fresh new approach. Extending the Grid with masonry is tempting but risks overcomplicating an already robust system. A standalone display: masonry module offers clarity but adds to CSS's learning curve.

### New Development: Item Flow (March 2025)

In March 2025, Apple's WebKit team proposed Item Flow, a new system that unifies concepts from Flexbox, Grid, and masonry into a single set of properties.

### Current Browser Support

No browser ships masonry layout unflagged in stable releases as of mid-2025. Firefox has had the longest-running flag-gated support, dating back to Firefox 77. Safari Technology Preview has been iterating on its own build.

# Masonry Layout CSS Syntax

Based on the MDN documentation, masonry layout uses the following CSS syntax:

## Display Property

**Exact syntax:** `display: grid-lanes` (or `display: inline-grid-lanes`)

From the documentation:
> "Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines **masonry layout** (also known as **grid-lanes** layout), which is accessed via the [`display`](/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Reference/Properties/display) values `grid-lanes` and `inline-grid-lanes`."

## Specification Level

**Specification:** CSS Grid Layout Module **Level 3**

From the documentation:
> "Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout"

## Example Implementation

```css
.grid {
  display: grid-lanes;
  gap: 10px;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(120px, 1fr));
}
```

## Key Point

The masonry behavior is controlled via the `display` property, not through `grid-template-rows: masonry` or `grid-template-columns: masonry`. Once `display: grid-lanes` is set, you define the grid axis using standard grid properties like `grid-template-columns` or `grid-template-rows`, and the other axis automatically uses masonry stacking.
```

## Current Status

In 2026, CSS Grid Lanes (formerly known as the masonry proposal) is the native browser answer — and Safari 26 just shipped it first. Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

## Syntax Evolution

The specification has undergone significant changes. The specification settled on the grid-lanes keyword rather than the earlier masonry proposal after years of debate between browser vendors. More specifically, a meeting was held on January 31st of 2025, and it was resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout.

The earlier syntax using `grid-template-rows: masonry` is no longer the standard. The Firefox-only grid-template-rows: masonry approach doesn't work anymore. It was dropped by CSSWG in favour of CSS Grid Lanes.

## Browser Support (Early 2026)

As of early 2026, CSS Grid Lanes is available in Safari 26 (first to ship). Chrome and Firefox have the feature behind experimental flags and are expected to ship stable support later in 2026. Supported in Safari 26.4+, Grid Lanes are also available as an experimental feature in Chrome and Firefox.

## Modern Implementation

To create the most common masonry layout where the columns are laid out in a grid, and the rows stack like masonry, use display: grid-lanes along with grid-template-columns.

# The "Just Use Grid" Option for CSS Masonry

## Core Proposal

According to the article, the "Just Use Grid" option adds minimal new syntax to CSS Grid. As stated:

> "The Just Use Grid option adds _only_ _one new value_ to CSS. You can create masonry-style layouts by simply using the `collapse` value in a track definition"

The implementation would look like changing a single property value: `grid-template-rows: collapse` (or `grid-template-columns: collapse` depending on direction).

## Key Tradeoffs and Objections

The article identifies several tradeoffs developers would face:

**Apparent complexity:** The Chrome team argues developers must write one additional line of code compared to a standalone masonry display type, though the WebKit team disputes whether this constitutes meaningful burden.

**Mental model switching:** According to the article, using separate masonry properties requires developers to "switch between mental models" and remember multiple sets of similar syntax with different names and defaults—a more significant cognitive load than writing extra code.

**Extensibility concerns:** The Chrome team believes segregating masonry from Grid allows specialized functionality development without coordinating across two systems. WebKit counters this could establish an undesirable precedent of creating separate display types for each new layout capability.
```
</details></tool-use>

# Masonry Layout Stacking Behavior

According to the MDN documentation, here is the exact verbatim description of how items behave on the stacking axis:

> "On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps."

This is the key characteristic that distinguishes masonry layout from regular grid layout. Instead of maintaining strict row alignment where shorter items leave empty space below them, items in subsequent rows actively move upward to fill any gaps created by taller items in previous rows, resulting in a tightly packed layout similar to traditional brickwork/masonry.
```
</details></tool-use>

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the CSS masonry syntax debate in 2025-2026:

## The Debate Resolved: Grid-lanes Won

The CSS Working Group resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout as of January 31st, 2025, and setting grid-lanes on the display property activates a masonry layout.

## The Two Competing Approaches

The debate centered on two competing syntaxes:

1. **`grid-template-rows: masonry`** - Mozilla and WebKit favored the grid-template-rows: masonry extension of existing grid, which is what Firefox and Safari have shipped behind flags.

2. **`display: masonry`** - The display: masonry version creates new opportunities and a simpler syntax.

## Why Grid-lanes Won

The CSS Working Group combined the two CSS masonry proposals into one draft specification with the hope that this would make it easier to compare the two and make a final decision.

## Current Browser Status

As of early 2026, CSS Grid Lanes is available in Safari 26 (first to ship), with Chrome and Firefox having the feature behind experimental flags and expected to ship stable support later in 2026.

## Alternative Proposal: Item Flow

In March 2025, Apple's WebKit team proposed Item Flow, a new system that unifies concepts from Flexbox, Grid, and masonry into a single set of properties, with Item Flow merging their strengths and replacing flex-flow and grid-auto-flow with a shorthand called item-flow.

## Gap Filling Behavior on the Stacking Axis

On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps. The child elements of this container will lay out item by item along the stacking axis according to the masonry algorithm: each row of items loads into the column with the most room, causing a tightly packed layout without strict row tracks.

## Current Implementation Status (2025-2026)

The masonry layout specification has evolved significantly. The specification settled on the grid-lanes keyword rather than the earlier masonry proposal after years of debate between browser vendors.

As of early 2026, browser support remains experimental and inconsistent across engines. The Chrome and Edge teams initially implemented Masonry in Chromium 140 as display: masonry but are already working on switching to grid-lanes.

## Browser Support Summary

Safari Technology Preview has one of the more complete prototype implementations. Firefox has implemented masonry experiments behind configuration flags. However, no major stable browser channel offers fully interoperable, unflagged support at the time of writing.

The most recent MDN documentation was last modified on Mar 9, 2026 by MDN contributors.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CSS masonry specification proposals in 2024-2025:

## Overview

The CSS Working Group published a First Public Working Draft of CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3 (CSS Masonry Layout) in September 2024, representing masonry layout with two possible syntactic forms: a grid-integrated syntax and a grid-independent syntax.

## The Two Main Proposals

**Grid-Integrated Syntax**: In this syntax, CSS masonry is directly integrated into CSS grid, with the masonry mode triggered by applying the keyword masonry to the grid-template-columns or grid-template-rows definition.

**Grid-Independent Syntax**: This syntax introduces CSS masonry as its own display type. A display value named masonry kicks off a flow-based system where items stack vertically by default, adjusting horizontally to fit the container, controlled with properties like masonry-direction and gap.

## Consensus and Evolution

The CSSWG resolved to adopt fully mixed track sizing for masonry layout, allowing all track listings expressible in CSS Grid for masonry as well, which unifies the two proposals' underlying layout models so both syntactic forms represent essentially equal capabilities.

The March 2025 update to Level 3 resolved key debates in masonry layout and incorporates the CSSWG's resolution to re-use grid-* properties for track templating and item placement.

## Additional Third Proposal

In March 2025, Apple's WebKit team proposed Item Flow, a new system that unifies concepts from Flexbox, Grid, and masonry into a single set of properties, replacing flex-flow and grid-auto-flow with a shorthand called item-flow.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CSS masonry syntax from webkit.org:

## Overview

The CSS Working Group has drafted a specification with two options for masonry syntax: the "Grid Integrated" option and the "Grid Independent" option.

## The Two Main Syntax Options

The specification presents the "Grid Integrated" option and the "Grid Independent" option going head-to-head.

**Grid Integrated Option:** Some people at Apple like having "Masonry" be part of CSS Grid, believing this functionality is a mechanism to expand CSS Grid to create columnar grids as well as modular grids, combined with all the other features of Grid, including the powerful options for defining columns, track spanning, explicit placement, and subgrid.

**Grid Independent Option:** The Chrome team believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed, with concerns around syntax, initial values, and how easy a version combined with grid would be to learn.

## Recent Update: Grid Lanes

More recently, WebKit introduced CSS Grid Lanes, with syntax like `display: grid-lanes; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(250px, 1fr)); gap: 16px;` This represents the evolved consensus from the CSS Working Group on how masonry layouts should work.

```text
# Masonry Layout - Key Details

## Definition of Masonry Stacking

The exact quote from the MDN documentation is:

> "Masonry layout is a layout method where one axis uses a typical strict grid layout, most often columns, and the other a **stacking** (masonry) layout. On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps."

## Spec Status & Browser Support

**Status: Experimental & Limited Availability**
- This is an experimental technology
- **Not Baseline** - does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers
- Part of **CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3** specification
- Accessed via `display: grid-lanes` or `display: inline-grid-lanes`

## How It Works Directionally

**Default Configuration (Column-based):**
```css
.grid {
  display: grid-lanes;
  gap: 10px;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(120px, 1fr));
}
```
- Columns are laid out in a strict grid
- Rows use masonry stacking (items rise to fill gaps)

**Alternative Configuration (Row-based):**
```css
.grid {
  display: grid-lanes;
  gap: 10px;
  grid-template-rows: repeat(3, 100px);
}
```
- Rows are laid out in a strict grid
- Items load into columns with masonry stacking

## Important Caveat

In browsers that don't support masonry, **regular grid auto-placement will be used as a fallback** instead.
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CSS grid masonry layout stacking axis gap filling behavior for 2025-2026:

## Stacking Axis Gap Filling Behavior

On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps. Each row of items loads into the column with the most room, causing a tightly packed layout without strict row tracks.

## Gap Property Behavior

On the masonry axis, gap controls the spacing between items as they stack, and the browser respects these values during its packing calculations. Gap behavior is consistent with standard grid on the defined axis.

## Current Specification Status

The specification has evolved significantly. The specification settled on the grid-lanes keyword rather than the earlier masonry proposal after years of debate between browser vendors. The CSS Grid Level 3 draft introduces masonry-style behavior, sometimes referred to as "grid lanes," where you define tracks on one axis (typically columns) and items pack tightly on the other axis using a masonry-style algorithm.

## Browser Support in 2025-2026

As of early 2026, browser support remains experimental and inconsistent across engines. As of early 2026, CSS Grid Lanes is available in Safari 26 (first to ship). Chrome and Firefox have the feature behind experimental flags and are expected to ship stable support later in 2026.

# Masonry Layout Syntax Change

## What Changed

The CSS Working Group moved away from grid-based masonry properties toward a dedicated display value. Rather than using `grid-template-rows: masonry` or creating an entirely separate `display: masonry` property, the official approach became `display: grid-lanes`.

## Current Official Syntax

The current official CSS syntax for masonry is:

```css
.masonry {
  display: grid-lanes;
}
```

According to the article, this represents a resolution to "re-use grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout" while establishing a distinct display keyword.

## When the Spec Changed

The definitive specification change occurred through two key meetings:

1. **January 31st, 2025**: A CSSWG meeting resolved to reuse grid properties for masonry
2. **Latest announcement** (referenced in the article): The `grid-lanes` keyword was officially adopted

The article notes that "deciding on the keyword alone took _years_," with numerous alternatives considered before settling on `grid-lanes`.

**Important caveat**: As of the article's publication date (December 2025), browsers have not yet fully implemented this standard. Chrome, Safari, and Firefox are currently working on transitioning from their existing masonry implementations to support `grid-lanes`.
```

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the CSS masonry specification and CSS Grid Lanes:

## Key Resolution (January 2025)

It was resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout. This decision came after the CSS Working Group debated for five years (2020-2025) about whether to integrate masonry into CSS Grid or create a separate module.

## Final Syntax: grid-lanes

Setting grid-lanes on the display property activates a masonry layout. Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

## How It Works

CSS Grid Lanes adds a new display mode that creates a masonry-style layout using the familiar grid syntax. Masonry layout is a layout method where one axis uses a typical strict grid layout, most often columns, and the other a stacking (masonry) layout. On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps.

## Browser Support (2025-2026)

In 2026, CSS Grid Lanes (formerly known as the masonry proposal) is the native browser answer — and Safari 26 just shipped it first. As of early 2026, CSS Grid Lanes is available in Safari 26 (first to ship). Chrome and Firefox have the feature behind experimental flags and are expected to ship stable support later in 2026.

## Specification Timeline

The specification published March 28, 2025 as CSS Grid Layout Level 3.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CSS grid-lanes and the masonry layout specification:

## Overview

Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

## What is Grid-Lanes?

Masonry layout is a layout method where one axis uses a typical strict grid layout, most often columns, and the other a stacking (masonry) layout. On the stacking axis, rather than sticking to a strict grid with gaps being left after shorter items, the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps.

## Basic Syntax

To create the most common masonry layout where the columns are laid out in a grid, and the rows stack like masonry, use display: grid-lanes along with grid-template-columns. The child elements of this container will lay out item by item along the stacking axis according to the masonry algorithm: each row of items loads into the column with the most room, causing a tightly packed layout without strict row tracks.

## Recent Developments

After a five-year debate, the CSS Working Group reached consensus in January 2025 to reuse Grid properties, unifying browser vendors around display: grid-lanes.

## Browser Support Status

As of early 2026, CSS Grid Lanes is available in Safari 26 (first to ship). Chrome and Firefox have the feature behind experimental flags and are expected to ship stable support later in 2026.

```text
# Chrome's CSS Masonry Proposal

**Chrome's Proposed Syntax:**
Chrome proposes a separate `display: masonry` value rather than extending grid. As stated in the article, "The Chrome team still believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed."

**Key Differences from the Alternative Approach:**

The Chrome proposal uses dedicated masonry properties:
- `masonry-template-tracks` for defining tracks
- `masonry-direction` for controlling flow (row, column, row-reverse, column-reverse)
- `masonry-track-*` for positioning items

The competing approach uses grid extensions:
- `grid-template-rows: masonry` or `grid-template-columns: masonry`
- `grid-auto-flow` for directional control
- Existing `grid-column-*`/`grid-row-*` properties

**Main Advantages of Chrome's Approach:**

The separate syntax offers better intuitiveness. "Changing the `display: masonry` version from columns to rows, means changing the value of `masonry-direction`," whereas the grid version requires switching property values and maintaining redundant specifications.

Additionally, Chrome's syntax provides cleaner defaults—`display: masonry` alone creates a functional layout without requiring explicit track definitions on non-masonry axes, improving developer experience and reducing complexity.
```
</details></tool-use>

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding CSS Grid Layout Level 3 and the grid-lanes specification:

## Official Specification and Terminology

Grid lanes layout has been added to CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3. The feature is documented in the official W3C CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3 specification.

## Official Names

Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

The feature is referred to by multiple names:
- **Grid Lanes** (primary name)
- **Masonry Layout** (alternative name)
- **Grid-lanes layout** (technical designation in the spec)

## Technical Description

Masonry layout lays out items into pre-defined tracks similar to grid layout in one axis (called the grid axis), but flows them freely similar to flex layout in the other (called the stacking axis).

## CSSWG Status

The CSS Working Group (CSSWG) continues actively developing this specification. Auto-placement uses the Grid Lanes Layout and Placement Algorithm, placing each item with an automatic grid position into the "shortest" track available.

The official specification is maintained at the W3C and is available at https://www.w3.org/TR/css-grid-3/ and https://drafts.csswg.org/css-grid-3/.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding the CSSWG resolution for CSS masonry syntax:

## CSSWG Final Decision on Masonry Syntax

In a meeting held on January 31st, 2025, the CSS Working Group resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout.

The decision was that setting `grid-lanes` on the display property activates a masonry layout, and it's going to be `display: grid-lanes`.

## Implementation Details

Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values `grid-lanes` and `inline-grid-lanes`.

To create the most common masonry layout where the columns are laid out in a grid and the rows stack like masonry, use `display: grid-lanes` along with `grid-template-columns`, and the child elements will lay out item by item along the stacking axis according to the masonry algorithm.

## Browser Support

The Firefox-only `grid-template-rows: masonry` approach was dropped by CSSWG in favour of CSS Grid Lanes, which are supported in Safari 26.4+ and also available as an experimental feature in Chrome and Firefox.

Based on the web search results, here's what has been resolved regarding the CSS masonry specification in 2025:

## Final Resolution

The debates about CSS masonry have been settled as of December 2025. On January 31st of 2025, it was resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout.

## The "Grid Integrated" Decision

The final decision leverages existing grid templating and placement properties, avoiding the creation of a new display value. This represents a victory for the "Grid Integrated" approach over the "Grid Independent" approach.

## New Name: CSS Grid Lanes

The newly named CSS Grid Lanes represents how the feature will work, and details are covered in "Introducing CSS Grid Lanes". Setting grid-lanes on the display property activates a masonry layout.

## Key Details

The early drafts of CSS Grid Level 3 contained two competing proposals. The February 2025 update incorporates the CSS Working Group's resolution to re-use the grid-* properties for track templating and item placement, and sketches out a new direction for controlling layout flow using a unified set of properties that would work across both Flex and Grid layout.

Chrome, Edge, Safari, and Firefox are already working on implementing grid-lanes.

# Chrome's Position on CSS Masonry Proposals

**Article Date:** September 19, 2024 (Last updated February 13, 2026)

## Chrome's Preference

Chrome favors the separate `display: masonry` syntax over the grid-based approach. The article states: "The Chrome team still believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed."

## Grid Approach Syntax

The grid-based method requires **more than just one new value**. It involves:

- Setting `grid-template-rows: masonry` (the new value)
- Defining `grid-template-columns` with track sizes
- Using `grid-auto-flow` for directional changes
- Potentially using `grid-template-areas`

As the article notes, "you always have to define tracks on the non-masonry axis" with grid, adding complexity beyond a single new keyword.

## Key Concerns with Grid Approach

Chrome identifies several issues with combining masonry into grid:
- Syntax complexity and inconsistency when switching between rows and columns
- Properties that become ignored or redundant
- Difficulty changing initial values in the future without breaking existing grid behavior

## Resolution Status

No final decision has been reached. The article invites developer feedback on GitHub Issue #9041 to help determine which approach should move forward.
```
</details></tool-use>

Based on the search results, I found comprehensive information about CSS masonry and the "grid-lanes" syntax resolution:

## CSSWG Resolution and Syntax

On January 31st of 2025, a meeting was held and it was resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout. The latest announcement is that setting grid-lanes on the display property activates a masonry layout.

## Official Syntax: `display: grid-lanes`

Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

## Basic Usage

To create the most common masonry layout where the columns are laid out in a grid, and the rows stack like masonry, use display: grid-lanes along with grid-template-columns. The child elements of this container will lay out item by item along the stacking axis according to the masonry algorithm.

Here's an example of the syntax:

```css
.grid {
  display: grid-lanes;
  gap: 10px;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fill, minmax(120px, 1fr));
}
```

## Key Design Decision

Deciding on the keyword alone took years, and there were many names that were considered leading up to grid-lanes. The resolution was to re-use grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout.

# Two CSS Masonry Specification Proposals

The article identifies two competing options for CSS masonry syntax:

## 1. "Just Use Grid" Option (Grid Integrated)
This approach extends CSS Grid with a new value for track sizing:
```css
.masonry {
  display: grid;
  grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
  grid-template-rows: masonry;
  gap: 10px;
}
```

## 2. "New Masonry Layout" Option (Grid Independent)
This creates a separate display type with dedicated properties:
```css
.masonry {
  display: masonry;
  masonry-template-tracks: repeat(3, 1fr);
  gap: 10px;
}
```

## Direct Quotes from Article

Regarding the options' conception, the article states:

> "Is this an extension of CSS Grid, leveraging existing Grid properties? Or should it be treated as something completely different, with its own set of new properties and new default values?"

On the Chrome team's position:

> "The Chrome team still believes that a separate masonry syntax would be the best way to proceed. While the biggest performance issue mentioned in our previous post is resolved, there are still concerns around syntax, initial values, and how easy a version combined with grid would be to learn."

The WebKit perspective emphasizes:

> "The WebKit team at Apple still strongly believes that separating CSS Grid and masonry-style packed layouts into two separate layout mechanisms would be a mistake."
```

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive status update on the CSS masonry specification:

## Current Status Update (2024-2025)

### Specification Development

The CSS Working Group published a First Public Working Draft of CSS Grid Layout Module Level 3 aka CSS Masonry Layout in September 2024. This draft represents masonry layout as a built-in capability of CSS in two possible syntactic forms: a grid-integrated syntax, and a grid-independent syntax, both of which are outlined as alternatives.

### Key Resolution

After proving it is possible, the CSSWG resolved to adopt mixed track-sizing for masonry-style layouts in September 2024. The CSSWG has resolved to adopt fully mixed track sizing for this layout model, allowing all the possible track listings expressible in CSS Grid Layout for masonry layout as well, which unifies the two incoming proposals' underlying layout models.

### Recent Evolution (2025)

As of December 2025, the debates about the grid-integrated versus grid-independent syntax have been settled, with the feature now known as CSS Grid Lanes. Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes.

### Browser Implementation

The Microsoft Edge and Google Chrome teams announced that CSS masonry is ready for early developer testing from Chrome and Edge 140.

### Outstanding Issues

There remain some major syntax questions open, including what display name to use for enabling masonry layout, and interpreting row vs column in masonry layouts.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about content density UI design with comfortable and compact modes in design systems:

## Definition

UI density is the amount of information or content shown within an area of a user interface, affecting how users perceive and interact with the interface and impacting readability and usability. In design systems, density is about creating flexible and adaptable UI components and layouts that allow the user to control how compactly the information is displayed.

## Comfortable and Compact Modes

Cloudscape supports two density modes: comfortable and compact, with comfortable being the standard density level active by default that optimizes content consumption and readability. Compact is an additional density level for data intensive views that increases the visibility of large amounts of data by reducing the space between elements on the screen.

Comfortable is described as the balanced default for mixed audiences with enough breathing room to feel calm and enough density to stay useful.

## Design System Implementation

Comfortable and compact modes are built using the 4px base unit of the spacing system. Density can be offered in multiple variants, like cozy, compact, and comfortable.

Spectrum's approach to density variations (comfortable, compact) and complex interaction patterns for creative tools is highly valuable for teams building sophisticated applications. This demonstrates current adoption in 2024-2025 design systems.

5. **Marketing/advocacy framing?** Yes. The "Just Use Grid" framing in the WebKit post is Apple's advocacy position in an active debate, not a neutral specification statement. Chrome's competing proposal (`display: masonry`) ultimately won, implemented as `display: grid-lanes`.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about CSS grid-lanes masonry specification and the CSSWG resolution:

## CSSWG Resolution (January 31, 2025)

On January 31st of 2025, the CSS Working Group held a meeting and resolved to reuse grid templating and placement properties for masonry layout. This resolution involved the CSS WG voting to reuse grid-* properties, adopting the display: grid-lanes syntax.

## Specification Details

Level 3 of the CSS grid layout specification defines masonry layout (also known as grid-lanes layout), which is accessed via the display values grid-lanes and inline-grid-lanes. To create the most common masonry layout where the columns are laid out in a grid, and the rows stack like masonry, use display: grid-lanes along with grid-template-columns.

## Publication and Implementation

The specification published March 28, 2025 as CSS Grid Layout Level 3. Safari leads with Technology Preview 234 (shipped December 19), and Chrome follows with experimental support in Chrome 140.

## Background of the Debate

The CSS Working Group debated for five years (2020-2025) about whether to integrate masonry into CSS Grid or create a separate module. Chrome initially prototyped display: masonry, Firefox tried grid-template-columns: masonry, and WebKit advocated for grid integration.

I found comprehensive information about content density in UI design. Here are the key findings:

## Definition

Content density is defined by the ratio of information visible compared to the space available in the interface. More specifically, information density refers to the amount of informational content in an interface and is measured by the proportion of information to available space.

## Density Modes

Several design systems implement density modes:

- **Cloudscape Design System**: Cloudscape supports two density modes: comfortable and compact. Comfortable is the standard density level of the system, active by default. It optimizes content consumption and readability, as well as cross device experiences. Compact is an additional density level for data intensive views. It increases the visibility of large amounts of data by reducing the space between elements on the screen.

- **Material Design**: Material Design's density scale includes three levels of density: default, comfortable, and compact.

- **SAP Fiori**: In compact mode, the font size remains the same, but the dimensions of both the controls and the spaces between them are smaller than in cozy mode.

## User Benefits

When you provide multiple density options, users can adapt the amount of content shown on the screen based on their needs and preferences. Some users might find it easier to complete their tasks by having larger spacing that increases distinction of content, while others might prefer smaller spacing and more content that is visible at a glance.

## Use Cases

Compact density can be great in data-rich applications that need to display a lot of information in a small amount of space. For example, data tables or long forms may benefit from an increase in density.

4. **Outdatedness**: The spec has evolved (the syntax shifted from `display: grid` + `grid-template-rows: masonry` to `display: grid-lanes`), but the *behavior description* — items on the stacking axis pack upward into gaps — remains accurate and unchanged across the evolution. This is the defining characteristic of masonry layout regardless of syntax era.

5. **Marketing/speculation**: MDN is not marketing material. This is a definitional statement about the algorithm's behavior.

The claim is accurately supported, from a high-quality primary source, and is not contradicted anywhere.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-07-13 20:00:47Z)**_

I have enough to make a determination. Let me analyze the checklist:

1. **Is the claim supported by the quote?** The quote says "the items in the following row rise up to fill the gaps." The claim says "items in later rows rise up to fill gaps left by shorter items." This is a near-paraphrase, but it introduces a subtle technical inaccuracy: masonry layout works **column-by-column** (each new item goes into the shortest column), not by literal "rows rising up." The MDN text uses "row" loosely as shorthand but the algorithm is actually a column-packing algorithm — items don't know about rows at all. The framing of "later rows" rising up misrepresents the mechanism even though MDN uses similar imprecise language.

2. **Contradicting evidence?** No source disputes the *behavior* (gaps get filled). However, the "rows" framing is imprecise — the CSS-Tricks article and MDN itself describe items going into "the column with the most room," not rows rising up.

3. **Source quality?** MDN is a primary, authoritative source for CSS. Good.

4. **Outdated?** The MDN page was last updated March 9, 2026. The specification has actually **renamed** the feature from `masonry` to `grid-lanes`, and the behavior description may reflect an earlier spec state. Browser support is still experimental/flagged in 2026.

5. **Marketing/speculation?** No.

The claim is directionally correct about the *outcome* (gaps get filled, no strict row alignment) but the mechanistic framing — "items in later rows rise up" — is a slight misread. The algorithm places each new item into the **shortest column**, it does not involve rows rising. MDN's own more careful description in the page body describes a column-packing algorithm, not row-rise behavior. The supporting quote, while present verbatim on MDN, is itself a simplification that can mislead about the actual mechanism. The claim reproduces that simplification without qualification. Given the instruction to default to `refuted=true` under uncertainty, and because the "rows rise up" framing is mechanistically imprecise even by MDN's own more detailed descriptions, this claim is borderline but not clearly refuted — the quote does support the surface-level description, and the behavior described is accurate even if the metaphor is loose.

On balance: the claim is **supported by the direct quote** from a high-quality primary source (MDN, updated March 2026). The mechanistic looseness ("rows rise up" vs. "column-packing") is an imprecision in MDN's own text, not an error introduced by the claim. The claim does not overreach beyond what the quote says.

The source page confirms the claim directly and completely. Now let me check the checklist items:

1. **Claim vs. quote match**: The claim says "two discrete modes are supported: 'comfortable' (default) and 'compact' (for data-intensive views)." The source says exactly that — comfortable is the default, compact is for data-intensive views. The definition of content density as a ratio is also verbatim from the source. No overreach.

2. **Contradicting evidence**: No credible source contradicts this. It is a design system specification from AWS/Cloudscape, not a contested empirical claim. The concept of comfortable/compact density is also confirmed by Material Design, IBM Carbon, and other major design systems using similar two-mode density patterns.

3. **Source quality**: Cloudscape Design is AWS's open-source design system with public documentation. This is a primary, first-party source for a claim about Cloudscape's own behavior. Source quality is entirely sufficient.

4. **Outdated**: Cloudscape is an actively maintained design system. The two-mode density pattern is a current, documented feature, not a legacy or deprecated claim.

5. **Marketing/speculation**: This is a design system specification page, not a marketing page or press release. It describes how the system actually works with precise technical detail (4px base unit, spacing reduction behavior). It is not speculative.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-07-13 20:00:47Z)**_

I now have sufficient evidence to render a well-grounded verdict. Let me analyze the claim precisely.

The claim states: "CSS masonry layout can be implemented using existing grid syntax by adding **only one new value** to the `grid-template-rows` or `grid-template-columns` property, **without requiring a new display type**."

Key findings:

1. **The WebKit article does say "only one new value"** — but the quote in the article itself is internally contradictory. The WebFetch of the primary source shows it describing `grid-template-rows: collapse` as the single new value, yet also shows the example using `grid-template-rows: masonry` (admitted as "name TBD"). This is a moving target, not a settled syntax.

2. **The "no new display type" part is refuted by the final CSSWG resolution**: The CSS Working Group resolved in January 2025 that masonry would use `display: grid-lanes` — a **new display type value** — not just a new value on `grid-template-rows/columns`. This directly contradicts the claim's "without requiring a new display type" component.

3. **Chrome's analysis explicitly contradicts the "only one new value" framing**: The grid approach requires users to also define tracks on the non-masonry axis, use `grid-auto-flow` for direction changes, and work around ignored/redundant properties — it is not a single-value addition in practice.

4. **The claim overstates the WebKit article's position**: The article was a call for feedback on competing proposals (Oct 2024), not a finalized standard. Treating its advocacy language as settled technical fact is a misread. The article itself is explicitly promotional for the "Just Use Grid" camp, written by a party (WebKit/Apple) with a stake in that outcome.

5. **The claim is now outdated**: The debate was resolved (CSSWG, Jan 2025) in favor of `display: grid-lanes`, which is a new display type — directly negating the "without requiring a new display type" clause.

_**Agent - sidechain (claude-sonnet-4-6 2026-07-13 20:00:59Z)**_

The primary source is confirmed and accessible. Now let me check the checklist items carefully.

**Checklist analysis:**

1. **Is the claim supported by the quote?** The supporting quote says: "Content density is defined by the ratio of information visible compared to the space available in the interface. Comfortable is the standard density level of the system, active by default. Compact is an additional density level for data intensive views." The claim closely paraphrases this. One minor imprecision: the claim says "two discrete modes are supported" — Cloudscape does support exactly two. The phrase "for data-intensive views" matches the source's "for data intensive views." This is a faithful paraphrase, not an overreach.

2. **Contradicting evidence?** The web search surfaces SAP Fiori using "Cozy and Compact" (not "comfortable"), Material Design using multiple density variants, and Basis Design System using its own terminology. These are design system competitors with different naming conventions — they do not contradict the Cloudscape-specific claim. No source disputes that Cloudscape uses exactly comfortable/compact with that definition.

3. **Source quality?** Cloudscape is Amazon Web Services' production design system, publicly documented. This is a primary source (the design system's own documentation). It is appropriate for a claim about Cloudscape's own behavior.

4. **Outdated?** Cloudscape is actively maintained. The terminology "comfortable/compact" is corroborated by 2024–2025 articles. No evidence of deprecation or change.

5. **Marketing claim?** This is design system technical documentation defining how their system works, not a marketing claim.

**One minor note:** The claim says "the ratio of information visible compared to the space available" — the source says "ratio of information visible compared to the space available in the interface." This is accurate. The claim is a faithful summary.

2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

## Synthesis: research report

**Question:** CSS grid mesh layouts for mod/card browsers — masonry, bento, asymmetric grids, card density patterns. Specifically for a City of Heroes mod browser with 291 mod cards showing name, category badge, download count, description, and download button. Looking for: modern CSS-only masonry approaches, bento grid patterns, card density toggles, featured/hero card layouts, and any UX research on card grid information hierarchy.

2 claims survived 3-vote adversarial verification. Merge semantic duplicates and synthesize.

## Confirmed claims
### [0] Masonry layout is an experimental technology not yet at Baseline, meaning it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers and should not be used in production without a fallback.
Vote: 3-0 · Source: https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Grid_layout/Masonry_layout (primary)
Quote: "This feature is not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers."
Verifier evidence (high): The MDN source directly states masonry layout has "Limited availability" and is "not Baseline because it does not work in some of the most widely-used browsers," matching the claim verbatim. The MDN page also explicitly labels it "experimental technology" and advises checking the compatibility table before production use. Independent corroboration: caniuse.com reports ~0% global usage with only Firefox 155+ showing stable support for `grid-template-rows: masonry`; Chrome/Edge 140 have it behind a flag; Safari stable does not yet ship it (only Safari 26 / Technology Preview does). A 2026 search confirms no major stable browser channel offers fully interoperable, unflagged support and the syntax is still evolving (earlier `masonry` track value vs. newer `display: grid-lanes` proposals are not yet aligned). The claim is current as of July 2026, the source is a primary authoritative reference (MDN), and the "should not be used in production without a fallback" recommendation is a direct, warranted inference from the documented experimental/limited-availability status — not an overreach. No credible source contradicts it.

### [1] There are two competing specification proposals for CSS masonry as of late 2024: a 'Grid Integrated' option (display: grid; grid-template-rows: masonry) and a 'Grid Independent' option (display: masonry; masonry-template-tracks: ...).
Vote: 2-0 · Source: https://webkit.org/blog/16026/css-masonry-syntax/ (primary)
Quote: "Currently, the specification is drafted with two options, going head-to-head — the 'Grid Integrated' option and the 'Grid Independent' option."
Verifier evidence (high): The claim is directly and accurately supported by the primary source. The WebKit blog post at webkit.org/blog/16026/css-masonry-syntax/ (published 2024) explicitly describes two competing proposals: a Grid Integrated option (display: grid; grid-template-rows: masonry) and a Grid Independent option (display: masonry; masonry-template-tracks). Multiple independent corroborating sources confirm this — the W3C CSS WG published the First Public Working Draft of CSS Grid Level 3 in September 2024 containing both options, Chrome DevRel published parallel coverage at developer.chrome.com/blog/masonry-syntax, and secondary analysis at CSS-Tricks, Smashing Magazine, and OddBird all confirm the two-proposal framing as of late 2024. The claim is time-scoped to "late 2024," which is accurate — the debate was genuinely live and unresolved at that time. By late 2025 the CSSWG resolved in favor of a grid-integrated approach renamed "CSS Grid Lanes" (display: grid-lanes), but that post-dates the claim's stated timeframe and does not retroactively make the 2024 characterization false. Source quality is high: webkit.org is a primary implementer and CSS WG participant, not a press release or forum speculation.


## Refuted claims (for transparency)
- "CSS masonry layout uses `display: grid-lanes` (not a `grid-template-rows: masonry` value), as it is defined in Level 3 of the CSS Grid Layout specification under the grid-lanes model." (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Grid_layout/Masonry_layout, vote 1-0)
- "On the masonry (stacking) axis, items in later rows rise up to fill gaps left by shorter items, rather than staying aligned to a strict grid row." (https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/CSS/Guides/Grid_layout/Masonry_layout, vote 1-0)
- "CSS masonry layout can be implemented using existing grid syntax by adding only one new value to the grid-template-rows or grid-template-columns property, without requiring a new display type." (https://webkit.org/blog/16026/css-masonry-syntax/, vote 0-1)
- "Content density is defined as the ratio of information visible compared to the space available in the interface, and two discrete modes are supported: 'comfortable' (default) and 'compact' (for data-intensive views)." (https://cloudscape.design/foundation/visual-foundation/content-density/, vote 0-0)
- "Comfortable mode must always be set as the default because compact mode can hinder readability, overwhelm users, and prolong content consumption." (https://cloudscape.design/foundation/visual-foundation/content-density/, vote 0-0)
- "Content Switcher is recommended for mutually exclusive view states (e.g., list vs. grid), not for filtering or navigation, which constrains its correct use in a mod browser density toggle." (https://carbondesignsystem.com/components/content-switcher/usage/, vote 0-0)
- "Card view design guidelines recommend using a gallery grid with built-in CSS responsiveness — cards expand and compress at breakpoints rather than using masonry or bento layouts." (https://www.patternfly.org/patterns/card-view/design-guidelines/, vote 0-0)
- "Card density toggle is supported via a toggle between card view and table/list view, not via a within-grid density slider." (https://www.patternfly.org/patterns/card-view/design-guidelines/, vote 0-0)
- "Card-based grid layouts consume more vertical space than list views, meaning a given screen cannot show as many cards as it could items in a list view, making density a direct trade-off against visual richness." (https://www.nngroup.com/articles/cards-component/, vote 0-0)
- "Cards are not effective for searching or comparing specific items; they work best for browsing heterogeneous or loosely related collections where ranking is deemphasized." (https://www.nngroup.com/articles/cards-component/, vote 0-0)
- "Cards function as summary entry points, not full-detail containers — they present 'a linked entry point to further details, rather than the full details themselves,' which directly shapes how much information should be surfaced per card." (https://www.nngroup.com/articles/cards-component/, vote 0-0)
- "Card view is visually engaging and creates effective groupings, while list view allows for easy sorting and is space efficient." (https://www.nngroup.com/videos/card-view-vs-list-view/, vote 0-0)
- "CSS Grid masonry (`grid-template-rows: masonry`) is experimental in Firefox Nightly only as of May 2025 and has no cross-browser support, making it unsuitable for production card browsers." (https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2025/05/masonry-css-should-grid-evolve-stand-aside-new-module/, vote 0-0)
- "The JavaScript grid hack for masonry uses `grid-auto-rows: 10px` combined with JS that calculates `gridRowEnd` spans from measured item heights, and requires recalculation on resize or content changes causing potential layout jumps." (https://www.smashingmagazine.com/2025/05/masonry-css-should-grid-evolve-stand-aside-new-module/, vote 0-0)
- "The shadcn bento grid uses a six-cell layout with varied col-span and row-span values to create visual weight and hierarchy across cells." (https://www.shadcn.io/blocks/hero-bento-grid, vote 0-0)
- "The bento grid is explicitly asymmetric — columns span different widths across cards — rather than a uniform grid." (https://www.shadcn.io/blocks/hero-bento-grid, vote 0-0)
- "Larger cells are used to emphasize featured/primary content while smaller cells carry secondary information, establishing a deliberate visual hierarchy." (https://www.shadcn.io/blocks/hero-bento-grid, vote 0-0)
- "CSS-native masonry layout can be achieved with a single property — `grid-template-rows: masonry` — requiring no JavaScript." (https://utilitybend.com/blog/masonry-with-css-grid-finally-a-solution-without-javascript, vote 0-0)
- "As of the article's 2021 writing, CSS grid masonry was only available in Firefox Nightly behind a feature flag (`layout.css.grid-template-masonry-value.enabled`), not in stable browsers." (https://utilitybend.com/blog/masonry-with-css-grid-finally-a-solution-without-javascript, vote 0-0)
- "A May 2023 update to the article notes the feature is still experimental, with separate Chrome and WebKit proposals still in progress — meaning production use remained blocked years after initial proposal." (https://utilitybend.com/blog/masonry-with-css-grid-finally-a-solution-without-javascript, vote 0-0)
- "Bento layouts with more than 12-15 visible cards lose their organizational advantage; an 8-card vs 16-card test showed the 8-card version had 31% lower time-to-target-action." (https://artofstyleframe.com/blog/css-layout-patterns-grid-flexbox-2026/, vote 0-0)
- "Bento grids fail on mobile because the visual hierarchy collapses into a stack of identical-width blocks." (https://artofstyleframe.com/blog/css-layout-patterns-grid-flexbox-2026/, vote 0-0)
- "CSS grid masonry layout can be achieved natively using `grid-template-rows: masonry`, but as of January 2021 this was experimental and only available in Firefox behind a feature flag." (https://griffadev.medium.com/building-a-responsive-progressively-enhanced-masonry-layout-with-only-css-and-html-998b2de89cf2, vote 0-0)

## Instructions
1. Identify claims that say the same thing — merge them, combine their sources.
2. Group related claims into coherent findings. Each finding should directly address the research question.
3. Assign confidence per finding: high (multiple primary sources, unanimous votes), medium (secondary sources or split votes), low (single source or blog-quality).
4. Write a 3-5 sentence executive summary answering the research question.
5. Note caveats: what's uncertain, what sources were weak, what time-sensitivity applies.
6. List 2-4 open questions that emerged but weren't answered.

Structured output only.
